To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether farmers along the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor, whose land has been fenced off to facilitate the new road, can include this land in their Single Farm Payment claim forms this year, given that there is no movement on the development of the new road.
answer_text: Previously impacted land may be declared as part of the current year’s application to the Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme, provided the land is available for agricultural use, farm businesses have management control, and land eligibility requirements are met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any analysis his Department has carried out of the impact that the draft Climate Action Plan 2023–2027 will have on the progression of major infrastructure projects in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 sets out policies and proposals from across Departments as required by legislation. All Government Departments progress their own projects using robust appraisal and analysis. Climate considerations ensure major projects are futureproofed and resilient to climate impacts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any funding that has been allocated for Areas with Natural Constraints payments over the next three years, as outlined in the Areas with Natural Constraints (Payments) Bill.
answer_text: It is for the Executive as a whole to agree the allocations to each department through the budget process.  Thereafter, it is for each Minister to prioritise their department’s spending within the budget provided to it by the Executive and endorsed by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Bill Sponsor in relation to funding for the Areas with Natural Constraints (Payments) Bill (NIA Bill 24/22-27).
answer_text: I haven’t had any discussions with the Bill Sponsor in relation to funding for the Areas with Natural Constraints (Payments) Bill.It is for the Executive as a whole to agree the allocations to each department during the budget process.  Thereafter, it is for each Minister to prioritise their department’s spending within the budget provided to it by the Executive and endorsed by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an updated assessment of the future of the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: I remain absolutely committed to the progression of the flagship A5WTC project. I recognise its strategic importance in helping tackle regional imbalance, improving the economy, job prospects and prosperity, better connecting communities, but most importantly the A5 is about improving road safety and saving lives. I am disappointed with the latest High Court’s conclusions in relation to the previously authorised section between South of Strabane and Ballygawley, however this represents only one step of the wider appeal process, which will now continue in the Court of Appeal.  I remain totally committed to pursuing the appeal in full. My priority remains to get the road built and to save lives.Work continues on the remaining two sections of the scheme, between New Buildings to south of Strabane and Ballygawley to the border, with close collaboration between both Administrations on the adjoining TEN-T and N2 infrastructure projects continuing.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions he intends to take to reassure the farming community that no two-tier system exists between farmers and his Department.
answer_text: From a farmed animal welfare point of view my Department treats all allegations in relation to animal welfare extremely seriously. All allegations are thoroughly investigated by my Department and enforcement action is taken in line with the DAERA enforcement policy.The same approach is taken in relation to environmental protection, where actions are taken in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he has taken to ensure proper care of all the animals on the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute farm in Hillsborough, including the treatment of any animals found to be sickly or injured by the veterinary service.
answer_text: AFBI is responsible for the welfare of all animals on the AFBI site at Hillsborough. This includes providing appropriate care for animals that are injured or unwell. DAERA’s Veterinary Service have responsibility for enforcement in respect of farmed animal welfare. In response to allegations of animal welfare issues in June 2025 and October 2025, Veterinary Service officials conducted unannounced inspections of the site and found no evidence of animal welfare issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of abortions that have been carried out in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department publishes an annual report on abortions carried out in Northern Ireland.The most recent publication on Northern Ireland abortion data was released on 2 December 2025 and can be accessed at the following link: https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/health/ni-abortion-stats-2024-25.html

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been carried out to determine the level of pollution which occurred at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute farm at Hillsborough, including whether this reached the watercourse.
answer_text: In October 2025 the NIEA investigated and confirmed that a polluting discharge was impacting a waterway, leading from the AFBI farm in Hillsborough. The incident was assessed as having a low severity environmental impact in accordance with the NIEA Water Pollution Severity Classification System. The NIEA will be continuing to carry out inspections of the waterway to ensure that pollution concerns remain fully addressed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the blowing of slurry into woodland at the Agri-Food and Biosciences farm in Hillsborough was illegal.
answer_text: I understand that the question may relate to historical video footage, the exact date of which is undetermined.The blowing of slurry / dirty water into woodland is not an approved spreading method under The Nutrient Action Programme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2019 and as such, is a contravention of the regulations.All reports of suspected contraventions of legislation are considered in line with DAERA’s Enforcement Policy.  In this case, the video footage has been assessed by NIEA staff.  Following that assessment and given the passage of time, NIEA officials have determined that there is insufficient credible, admissible evidence to consider taking forward a prosecution in this instance.  This underlines the need for timely reporting of suspected non-compliance, as it allows inspectors to promptly visit the site to collect or verify evidence for enforcement.The NIEA will continue to monitor compliance at this and other sites to protect the water environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the date on which he first became aware of issues at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute farm at Hillsborough.
answer_text: I was aware of concerns being raised through an MLA in relation AFBI in November 2024. These did not relate to animal health or environmental compliance issues and were passed to the AFBI Chief Executive to deal with. In June 2025 my Department was made aware by the Chief Executive that concerns in relation to animal welfare issues at Hillsborough had been received and that these were being examined.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of expertise his Department requires, in relation to the 23 vacancies in the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
answer_text: A significant level of scientific expertise is required for posts in the NIEA’s planning consultee teams.  The scientific disciplines relevant to providing environmental advice on planning consultations include ecology and biodiversity, water environment (hydrology and hydrogeology), air quality, environmental quality and pollution and marine and aquatics.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps is he taking to address the 23 staff vacancies in the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
answer_text: The staff vacancies related to providing environmental advice on planning consultations are being treated as a priority for filling from the scientific recruitment competitions being run by the NICS.  These recruitment competitions are progressing at various stages and it is anticipated that the majority of vacancies will be filled over the next 6 months. In the interim, and to ensure continuity of service, posts are being filled using temporary promotions and agency staff where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the delays that elected representatives have in receiving responses from the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, how he can ensure that they receive responses in relation to bovine TB in good time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the target time for the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to respond to a query from an MLA with a full response and not simply an acknowledgement.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) aims to provide timely and substantive replies to correspondence on planning consultations from MLAs. While an initial acknowledgement is issued upon receipt; from 1 March 2026, responses to MLAs now include information on average processing times for the relevant type of development.NIEA is transitioning to providing full, substantive updates on planning consultations to all MLA queries by 1 April 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle that have been removed from farms as a result of bovine TB in (i) 2025; and (ii) 2026.
answer_text: The numbers of animals removed from farms in 2025 and in January 2026 are broken down into those cases reported due to a reaction to the Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (CITT) known as the skin test; those which were reported due to a positive result from the Interferon gamma (IFN-g) blood test which is carried out in addition to the skin test in some bTB breakdown herds; and those subject to removal as negative in contact (NIC) animals considered to be at high risk of disease even though they may has tested negative. NIC animals may be animals in a batch of cattle where there has been a high percentage of reactor cattle, or animals taken as part of a herd depopulation in severe disease outbreaks. The test positive animals are recorded by date of the positive test, and the NIC animals are recorded by date of death. For 2025 and 2026 the numbers of CIIT positive animals are up to date until the end of November 2025. December 2025 and January 2026 figures have not yet been validated. These figures will be updated on the DAERA website when confirmed and can be found at this link: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland-2007-feb-25Animals removed in 2025*CITT reactors up to end of Nov 2025IFN-g test reactorsNegative in Contact animals*Total *177391379426*19544* CITT Reactors up to end of Nov 2025 In January 2026 CITT reactorsIFN-g test reactorsNegative in contact animalsTotalNumbers not yet validated13725-

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment he has made of the backlog of delays within the planning system, as a direct result of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency not responding to the consultant process in a reasonable time frame.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) acknowledges that delays in issuing consultation responses have impacted the wider planning system. I recognise the frustration that these delays have caused for planning authorities, applicants, and other stakeholders.NIEA continues to experience very heavy workloads alongside sustained resource pressures. I am aware of these ongoing constraints and can confirm that plans are in place to fill vacancies through current scientific recruitment competitions. These additional resources are intended to support sustained improvement in our consultee performance.Current planning performance indicates that 86% of planning consultations received in 2025 /2026 have been responded to. However, I acknowledge that DAERA is not meeting its statutory target and that there is need for improvement.Reducing the backlog and improving timeliness remains a priority. A range of measures are being progressed through the NIEA Planning Improvement Plan to drive and embed better performance across our planning consultation functions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any actions he has taken to address the increase in bovine TB cases.
answer_text: The high level of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland, while showing a slight decline in the last quarter, is challenging and unacceptable.  Herd incidence for the 12 months to December 2025 was 9.90% compared with 10.70% for 2024. To address this I established the TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) in January 2025 to accelerate the eradication of bTB. Comprising industry, veterinary, and wildlife stakeholders, the group developed a "Blueprint for Eradication" which I endorsed and published in April 2025. This blueprint outlines our strategic direction across three pillars of cattle, wildlife and people, and which are now being supported by new departmental structures to advance progress as quickly as possible. My department is currently working with stakeholders to implement this blueprint. In terms of actions being taken forward to address the high level of bovine TB here, work planned includes further increasing the use of interferon gamma testing (depending on funding availability); launching a scheme for the provision of vet-led on-farm biosecurity advice; efforts aimed at improving TB testing; and ongoing preparations for a new consultation on wildlife intervention options. Following this, I will be able to decide on next steps for the wildlife intervention measures to be implemented alongside people and cattle measures as part of the overall bTB blueprint for eradication strategy.In addition to the priorities outlined above, the Department will continue to advance the innovative regionalisation initiative in partnership with the Republic of Ireland.This is the first time a regionalised approach to this disease will be trialled on the island of Ireland and will see a co-ordinated package of measures undertaken. This is expected to include further research into the use of a test, vaccinate or remove approach to wildlife intervention; enhanced cattle testing; strengthened movement controls; and improved on-farm biosecurity. The findings generated through this innovative research initiative will provide important evidence to inform future bovine TB eradication policy on both sides of the border.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any action his Department has taken to address sickness absence across the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: The health and wellbeing of colleagues and the reduction of sickness absence levels is a priority for all departments. The latest NISRA statistics for 2024/25 show that 57.6% of staff have had no sickness absence in that period. The same report notes a decrease in absence rates of 0.4 average working days lost from the previous year, which is to be welcomed. However, I recognise the significant impact of ill-health and sickness absence on individuals and teams, the public purse and the quality of service delivery.  My officials remain committed to working with departments, Trade Unions and other partners to support people to stay in work and return to work as soon as they are able to, in order to provide the best possible public service. We are continually analysing the reasons for absence, targeting interventions, and supporting departments to manage absences in line with statutory and policy requirements. 	Anxiety, stress, depression and other psychiatric illnesses are the largest reason for absence in the NICS. Given the prevalence of mental health issues, both in the workplace and wider society, my department offers a comprehensive range of interventions for staff. This includes access to an in-house welfare support service, access to free, 24 hour counselling, and wellbeing and mental health awareness training for staff and managers. The Civil Service is also placing an increased focus on our occupational health service to improve the health and wellbeing of staff. The Civil Service Sickness Absence policies are currently under review as part of the HR policy renewal strand of the People Strategy (2025-30). This review will focus on prioritising early intervention and employee-centred support with the aim of preventing illness where possible, whilst developing effective and equitable procedures for managing sickness absences when they occur.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the Memorandum of Understanding between the Government of the United States of America and the UK Government for Securing of Supply in the Mining and Processing of Critical Minerals and Rare Earths, signed on 4 February 2026; and (ii) any opportunities her Department has identified for Northern Ireland to contribute in sustainable critical minerals (a) supply chains; (b) research; (c) investment; and (d) processing activities, that align with this Memorandum of Understanding and support local economic g
answer_text: The Memorandum of Understanding was signed between the British Government and the US Government; my Department was not involved in this Memorandum of Understanding. My officials have sought clarification from the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) regarding any impact this Memorandum of Understanding may have here.				My Department has a number of research and evidence-gathering programmes in place concerning critical minerals. Some of this work forms part of my Department’s ongoing Review of Mineral Licensing, alongside research into recycling, the circular economy, and wider supply chain resilience.				My officials will continue to engage with DBT through the Critical Minerals Forum to assess how the Memorandum of Understanding relates to our own environmental obligations and policy frameworks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any proposals his Department has to recognise the historic significance of Strabane native, John Dunlap and his role in printing the first copies of the Declaration of Independence in 1776.
answer_text: John Dunlap’s significant contribution will be recognised as the first printer of the Declaration of Independence. I am keen to highlight the historic role played by people from Northern Ireland to the foundation of the United States of America.John Dunlap will feature in the upcoming Public Record Office of Northern Ireland flagship exhibition to be held from June to November 2026 in Titanic Quarter, Belfast; and a PRONI Ulster American Treasures publication to mark the 250th anniversary. The exhibition and publication will both feature original documents from PRONI collections including an original letter from John Dunlap written to his family back in Strabane in 1785. I was also pleased to meet with you recently in relation to the potential for a statue / memorial in Strabane. I would like to express my support for your proposal to recognise John Dunlap’s legacy in Strabane.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any progress that has been made on reducing bed blocking in hospitals in the last 12 months.
answer_text: There is ever increasing pressure on our hospital services. This is exacerbated by the delayed discharges of patients medically fit to leave hospital and as of 30 January 2026, there were 585 patients delayed (Source: SPPG Information).Through the Winter Preparedness Plan, urgent actions have been implemented across hospitals, community services and social care to reduce these pressures.Improving discharge processes, including sevenday working, is central to improving patient flow. Trusts are strengthening Early Review Teams, discharge coordination and allied health professional capacity to support timely senior decisionmaking and reduce avoidable delays. Regional consistency has also been implemented for the protection of care packages during hospital admission, ensuring packages are available immediately when patients are medically fit for discharge. Digital solutions are strengthening capacity management, including Careline Live, now live across all five Trusts, providing realtime oversight of home care capacity. The Trusted Assessor model has now been introduced across all Trusts to support timely discharge to care homes. All Trusts are represented on a Providers Collaborative Forum and, with the acknowledgement that one of the main challenges affecting discharge and patient flow, is lack of dementia care home capacity, the forum has proposed a regional enhanced dementia care home tariff.  There is currently ongoing engagement with care home providers in an effort to secure additional dementia care home capacity.Additional shortstay inpatient beds and intermediate care beds are supporting stepdown care, alongside expanded Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respiratory, cardiology and surgical specialties. Frailty at the Front Door services are also in place to improve early decisionmaking for older patients and avoid unnecessary admissions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.
answer_text: I can advise that 752 of the 835 applicants who submitted an Expression of Interest have now been invited to complete a Stage Two application to the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund.   The closing date for applicants to submit their Stage Two application was Friday 23 January 2026 with 521 applications received.   Scoring and assessment of submitted applications is now underway, and it is currently planned that applicants will be notified of their outcomes by the end of March 2026.  Funded projects will then be delivered within the 2026/27 financial year. Due to the high level of interest in the Fund and the clear need for intervention within the sector, I am keen to see this pilot scheme become a longer term programme and have instructed my officials to bring forward proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what her Department has delivered for the people in West Tyrone, since taking office.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, my Department has delivered a significant number of important infrastructure improvements across West Tyrone. My overriding priority, however, remains the progression of the flagship A5 Western Transport Corridor project. This scheme is of strategic importance to the people of West Tyrone, helping to address regional imbalance, strengthen the economy, enhance connectivity between communities, and significantly improve road safety.My Department has also delivered a substantial programme of improvements to the road network in West Tyrone. Full details of these improvements are set out in the most recent Council Reports for Fermanagh & Omagh District Council and Derry City and Strabane District Council, which are available here: Council Reports | Department for Infrastructure. As well as the improvements detailed within the Council reports, my Department continues to develop proposals for the A32 Cornamuck road improvement scheme. In June 2025 I announced that the scheme was to progress to the procurement and construction stage. The Department will now commence work to review and update the necessary documents to bring this scheme to procurement, including the finalising of the Business Case. It is currently projected that this scheme will commence procurement in 2030/31 and construction in 2031/32.My Department also continues to make progress in delivering active travel initiatives. Across West Tyrone, and a number of projects have been supported through collaborative working with other departments under the COVID Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Scheme Programme, the location of these projects, by Council area, are as follows:Derry City and Strabane District Council- Vaughan’s Holm, Newtownstewart; new paths, widening of the existing riverside path and additional seats and signage.- Sion Mills: Environmental improvements in the public realm space at Main Street.- Castlederg: Installation of lighting to the recently developed greenway network in the town and improvements to active travel infrastructure at Castle Park. Fermanagh and Omagh District Council- Drumquin - planting, landscaping, parking improvements and creation of a safe and accessible space for residents and the wider community to use. - Trillick: delivery of new walking facilities around the sports pitch.I have also committed to the delivery of the Strabane pedestrian / cycle bridge scheme which is included within the Derry City and Strabane District Council’s Growth Deal. My officials have recently appointed an Engineering Consultant to progress the scheme with development to commence in January 2026. Additionally, my Department has supported the delivery of cross-border greenway projects which were delivered through the auspice of the INTERREG VA programme, including Castlefinn-Ballymagorry via Lifford and Strabane.My Department continues to also invest in flood alleviation infrastructure in West Tyrone and is curren

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what his Department has delivered for the people in West Tyrone, since taking office.
answer_text: My Department’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme has spent £17.49m over the past two years across Northern Ireland. Regional initiatives such as the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme, the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme, the Rural Community Development Support Service have ensured the sustainability and growth of rural voluntary and community organisations and micro-businesses in West Tyrone while people across the area can avail of support services provided through the Rural Support Charity, including Social Farming, and the Farm Families Health Checks Programme.Since February 2024, the Water Quality Improvement Strand of the Environment Fund has supported nine projects to the value of £203k in West Tyrone. The Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP) has recently expanded to the Owenkillew River, which is Special Area of Conservation. To date, some £145k of grant support has been provided to farm businesses in West Tyrone to implement measures designed to protect and improve water quality. Farmers in the SCP have also received advisory support from The Rivers Trust and an individual Farm Water Environment Management Plan.My Department has funded 137 farms in West Tyrone, providing capital investment under Tier 1 and Tier 2 of the Farm Business Improvement scheme to a value of £2.35m to date. A further £90m of subsidy and grant payments have been provided to support farmer(s)/farm businesses in the area via a range of Agricultural and Environmental Schemes including:the Basic Payment Scheme / Farm Sustainability Transition Paymentthe Beef Carbon Reduction SchemeEnvironmental Farming SchemeForestry SchemesProtein Crop SchemeUnder the Sustainable Agriculture Programme my Department has launched the Farming for Sustainability Knowledge Scheme and the Farming for Generations Scheme. The former supports 233 farmers in West Tyrone through the delivery of peer to peer learning groups. Information and awareness events delivery under this scheme are also open to all farmers in this area.The Farming for the Generations Pilot Scheme has raised awareness of succession planning within Northern Ireland and provided practical support to 24 farm businesses in West Tyrone. The Northern Ireland Regional Food Programme also supported Fermanagh and Omagh Christmas Farmers’ Markets with £4,711.20 of support. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what his Department has delivered for the people in West Tyrone, since taking office.
answer_text: As with all areas of Northern Ireland, West Tyrone receives core education services and funding, including routine support from the Education Authority (EA) to schools and education settings. In addition, a range of targeted investment and delivery has been directed specifically to the West Tyrone area by my Department.In relation to Early Years provision, Sure Start services are delivered within at least the 25% most disadvantaged Super Output Areas in Northern Ireland, as defined by the Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure (2017). In West Tyrone, services are delivered by the LAST, Rainbow and Strabane Sure Start projects. Investment has increased substantially since I took office, from £2.5m in 2024/25 to £2.8m in 2025/26.Through the Pathway Fund, 31 Early Years settings in West Tyrone benefited from increased funding of £639,520 in 2024/25 and £797,622 in 2025/26. In recognition of rising delivery costs, I increased the maximum Pathway award from £30,000 to £40,000 from 2025/26.Fourteen preschool settings in West Tyrone are participating in Cohorts One and Two of my PreSchool Standardisation Programme, which aims to standardise session lengths to a minimum of 22.5 hours per week. Seven settings transitioned in September 2025, providing an additional 146 fulltime places, with a further 208 places to be delivered when Cohort Two transitions in September 2026.Since September 2024, 1,310 children and 917 households in West Tyrone have benefitted from £934,700 in subsidies from the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.Almost £1 million of Extended Schools funding has been provided to over 30 schools in West Tyrone since 2024, supporting provision outside of the school day in disadvantaged communities.To support teachers, my Department has invested £31m through the Teacher Professional Learning Fund. All postprimary schools have been invited to participate in the Continued Professional Development Academy, and two West Tyrone schools supported Research Informed Conferences under the Making Best Practice, Common Practice programme.In February 2025, I published the SEN Reform Agenda and fiveyear Delivery Plan, with priority actions progressing using funding from the Public Sector Transformation Board and the Early Learning and Childcare budget. In West Tyrone, four settings are participating in the Early Years Assistants SEN Training project, while seven preschool settings received support from the PreSchool Inclusion Fund in 2024/25 and a further 44 in 2025/26.I have approved a number of Development Proposals in West Tyrone, including new nursery units at Donemana Primary School and Omagh Integrated Primary School, an Irishmedium nursery unit at St Colmcille’s Primary School, and an increase in intake at St Dympna’s Primary School.In 2024/25, my Department delivered £6.6 million of capital investment in West Tyrone, including £4.9 million through Minor Works and £1.6 million through Major Works to progress new school buildings. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the recommendations made by the Tuberculosis Partnership Steering Group will be placed in the public domain.
answer_text: The recommendations made by the TB Partnership Steering Group, in collaboration with my department, are set out in the Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication.  I was pleased to endorse the Blueprint which was published in April 2025.  The Blueprint sets out the key priorities and actions to be further developed to tackle this disease and clearly identifies those measures where progress must be made in the immediate future across the three pillars of people, cattle and wildlife.Further information can be found here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any recommendations he has received from the Tuberculosis Partnership Steering Group since it was established on 29 January 2025.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) recommendations to tackle Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) are set out in the Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication which was published on 15th April 2025. Further information can be found here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradication.My department is now working closely with TBPSG to advance the agreed short-, medium- and long-term objectives set out in the Blueprint.  As part of that process, my officials, led by the Chief Veterinary Officer, meet regularly and receive feedback from TBPSG members to assist in the progress and implementation of these actions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any increase that has occurred in bovine TB cases, since he took office in February 2024.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) disease rates have been consistently high in Northern Ireland in recent years, with the annual herd incidence rate fluctuating between 10.05% and 10.70% since 2022.  The long-term trend has been on an upward trajectory for many years, and other jurisdictions are also grappling with recent rises in disease rates.For the 12 months to the end of February 2022, herd incidence stood at 10.02% and animal incidence at 1.00% (these figures were published in April 2024).  Currently, for the 12 months to the end of October 2025, herd incidence is 10.60% and animal incidence is 1.132%.  Clearly these figures are unacceptably high. That is why I have made it a top priority of my department to work alongside the TB Partnership Steering Group, which I established in January 2025, to bring forward new measures aimed at tackling the disease as agreed in the Group’s Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication. The Blueprint was published on my department’s website in April 2025 and can be accessed at:  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when farm businesses can expect to see a reduction in bovine TB cases following any intervention his Department has put in place.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication sets out clear targets for the eradication of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland (NI). Further information can be found here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradicationThe aim of the TBPSG, working with DAERA, is to reduce bTB herd incidence by 2% by 2030, placing NI on a pathway to halve current bTB levels by 2040 and achieve eradication by 2050. This is an ambitious target and progress achieved towards this target will become clearer over the time ahead as new measures (as outlined in the Blueprint) are implemented and become part of the overall Northern Ireland bTB Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farm businesses affected with bovine TB that he has engaged with, since he took office in February 2024.
answer_text: Since taking up post in February 2024, I have met with a wide range of businesses, stakeholders, farmers, interest groups, other governments, and industry bodies that are impacted by, or have an interest in, bovine Tuberculosis (bTB).   In addition, I established the TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) in January 2025, which includes experts from farming, industry, environmental, and veterinary fields. I was pleased to endorse its Bovine TB IN Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication which was published in April 2025 and can be found here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradicationTackling bTB is one of my key priorities and I have heard too much from all my engagement to date, of the devastating impact bTB has on farm families and their businesses.  The Blueprint represents a new way forward based on close collaboration and joint working to bring forward measures aimed at finally tackling this costly disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the preparations her Department has made to deal with frost and snow over the winter period.
answer_text: I am pleased to advise that the delivery of winter service functions within my Department commenced on 1 October 2025. Throughout the winter season, approximately 300 staff and 130 gritters are available and ready to salt main roads in order to help drivers across the North deal with the wintry conditions.Our salt barns are stocked with 55,000 tonnes with reserve stocks of around 20,000 tonnes available. I have also made provision for further supplies, at the rate of around 15,000 tonnes per month if required. My Department has a Snow Contingency Plan, which ensures that, initially, efforts are directed towards clearing snow from motorways and the trunk roads before moving on to other main roads and the busiest urban link roads. My Department also has arrangements in place with contractors and farmers to help clear blocked roads in the event of heavy snowfalls.Whilst the Department targets the limited resources available for gritting towards the busier through routes, on many other routes which do not qualify for inclusion in the gritting schedule, over 5,600 salt bins and 52,000 grit piles are provided for use by the public on a self-help basis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the number of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme since 1 January 2025.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board have received over 12,200 applications since the scheme opened on the 31st of August 2021, with 1,707 applications received since 1st January 2025. To date, over £116 million has been paid to eligible applicants in recognition of the harm they have suffered.   With the application process due to close on 30 August 2026, we strongly encourage anyone who may be eligible to submit an application. Support is available through a number of groups, including WAVE Trauma Centre, SEFF, Relatives for Justice, the Ely Centre and Ashton Community Trust, for anyone who needs help with the application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department has put in place to monitor the impact on the (i) veterinary sector; (ii) farming community; and (iii) agricultural business, from 1 January 2026, when EU rules will apply to the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the supply of veterinary medicinal products into Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. The Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs [Defra]) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.   	I remain committed to ensuring the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland, safeguarding animal health and welfare, while supporting vets, farmers, and the agri-food industry.I regularly engage with the UK Government on supply arrangements from 1 January 2026, raising concerns directly with Emma Reynolds MP, Defra Secretary of State, and holding frequent bilateral meetings with Baroness Hayman of Ullock, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at Defra, where veterinary medicines supply is regularly discussed.I sit on the UK Government’s Veterinary Medicines Working Group, co-chaired by Baroness Hayman, alongside veterinary experts, industry representatives, and political stakeholders. This forum enables me to advise on measures to prevent disruption to supply and minimise impacts on vets, farmers, and the agri-food industry.I welcome the UK Government’s Protecting Animal Health policy (June 2025), and its two new support schemes designed to address supply challenges, including unforeseen issues. In the months ahead, I will continue working with the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group to ensure these schemes deliver their intended outcomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work he is doing with the Ulster Beekeepers' Association to manage short-term risk until a fully trained bee inspector service is in place.
answer_text: My Department’s legislative obligations in relation to honeybee health are carried out by appropriately trained staff from within the Agriculture Inspectorate. Department officials regularly meet with the Ulster Beekeepers Association (UBKA) to share information on disease risks and occurrences in Northern Ireland, guidance for beekeepers and recruitment. Through this collaboration, UBKA have been proactive in communicating honeybee disease matters to their members. This includes the importance of beekeepers’ vigilance, bio security and import requirements and controls to reduce risk of disease introduction. UBKA also promote the use of DAERA funded Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) honeybee disease diagnostic and testing services to their members.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has (i) funded; and (ii) authorised, any part of the legal defence or representation arising from the neurology recall cases.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department holds or administers Crown Indemnity for consultants employed by the (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) all other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department holds Crown Indemnity for Health and Social Care medical and dental staff employed across our HSC covering negligence (acts or omissions) of HSC staff in the course of their employment. Crown Indemnity does not extend for claims arising from a consultant's private practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department meets their statutory duty in the area of bee inspections with no inspectors in post.
answer_text: My Department does not employ dedicated bee inspectors, however, it recruits Agriculture Inspectors and provides training in bee inspections to meet its legislative obligations. Under the Bee Diseases and Pests Control Order (Northern Ireland) 2007, it is the obligation of the beekeeper to monitor their apiaries and notify the Department should they know or suspect the presence of a notifiable disease or pest. This is then confirmed through a diagnostic and testing service at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) and is available for all beekeepers to submit samples for testing, which is fully funded by the Department. If a notifiable disease or pest is confirmed, follow-up action on the apiary will be notified and directed by an appropriate Departmental official.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail whether any promotions will be made from the temporary senior prison officers list within the next (i) six months; and (b) 12 months.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service has no plans to progress the remaining candidates from the last Senior Officer temporary promotion competition, which will expire in March 2026.  Work is progressing to develop proposals for a new style Senior Officer, Governor Unit Manager and Governor Functional Head internal promotion competition, that is in keeping with the revised assessment and selection process developed for new entrant Custody Prison Officers which launched in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department has put in place to prevent a major outbreak of foulbrood or other bee diseases.
answer_text: My Department facilitates and funds a honeybee disease testing and diagnostic service by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). If a beekeeper suspects the presence of a notifiable disease in their apiaries, they are obligated to provide samples to confirm its presence. If a notifiable disease is confirmed, follow-up action on the apiary will be notified and directed by a DAERA official.Contingency plans are in place to deal with significant outbreaks, including the use of staff from across DAERA and from other organisations as required. DAERA continues to monitor changes to risk profiles, including from bee diseases and undertakes inspections accordingly.The Member will be aware from previous briefings that the movement of honeybees into and out of Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).  I understand that action to ensure compliance with relevant legislative requirements continues to be taken to mitigate the risk of the introduction of notifiable pests and diseases.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why there is currently no bee inspectors in post.
answer_text: My Department does not employ dedicated bee inspectors, however, it recruits Agriculture Inspectors and provides training in bee inspections to meet its legislative obligations. At present, three DAERA staff undertake bee inspection work as necessary within their overall inspectorate duties. DAERA provides beekeepers with testing and diagnostic services through the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made by his Department, on the impact the absence of bee inspectors has on beekeeping.
answer_text: My Department does not employ dedicated bee inspectors, however, it recruits Agriculture Inspectors and provides training in bee inspections to meet its legislative obligations. 	My officials are continuing to deliver legislative requirements regarding bee inspections, and support apiary owners and associations in promoting bee health through published guidance and engagement at events where possible. Beekeepers are urged to remain vigilant when managing their apiaries and avail of the testing and diagnostic services provided by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) on behalf of the Department to ensure that honeybee disease levels remain at a low level in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the control and restraint expertise that an individual from Professional Standards has (i) in assessing whether a case should be referred to Professional Standards; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made as to whether this may constitute a conflict of interest.
answer_text: (i) The Professional Standards Unit (PSU) receives referrals from a commissioning manager within an establishment, once a fact-finding exercise has been completed, in line with the Professional Code of Conduct (PCOC).(ii) The investigators for disciplinary matters in the PSU are trained and experienced Governor grades.  This includes experience in all operational matters, including Use of Force procedures and its application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to reduce the waiting times for women red flagged for breast screening, given that it is currently 12 to 14 weeks over the targeted waiting time of two weeks, as set by his Department.
answer_text: I can confirm that £5 million of recurrent funding has been identified under the Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation and Funding Plan to support and expand breast services across Health and Social Care Trusts.  This funding is being directed towards targeted initiatives designed to alleviate service pressures and improve patient access.Funding is currently being used to commission additional breast assessment and treatment capacity via a combination of in-house and independent sector whilst work is ongoing towards increasing core HSC capacity using more sustainable and efficient care pathway models.  In October 2025, a bespoke pilot was introduced to address immediate pressures in breast services and inform longer-term elective care reform. This pilot adopts a Payment by Results model, remunerating consultants based on the number of patients seen rather than traditional sessional hours.  Building on successful practice within Western Health & Social Care Trust, this approach aims to unlock additional HSC capacity for red flag breast assessment clinics.  Key benefits include alignment with long-term elective care reform goals, encouraging efficient, outcome-driven care delivery, and offering flexibility and sustainability beyond traditional sessional models.  My Department is working closely with Trusts to ensure Red Flag referrals are prioritised within existing core capacity to ensure timely access for the most clinically urgent cases.  Following introduction of the bespoke Payment by Results model, a number of additional regional waiting list clinics were delivered during evening and weekends throughout October.  Further extra clinics have been confirmed for all Trusts in November 2025 and December 2025.These measures, combined with increased core activity post-summer, have contributed to a reduction in waiting times for red flag breast assessment from 10 weeks to 7 weeks.  I still believe that is too long but good progress is being made to bring the waiting times down further. Planning is also underway for additional clinics into early 2026 to maintain this progress. My Department is working with key stakeholders to implement sustainable, long-term solutions.  A regional workshop is scheduled for 26 November to explore service redesign opportunities, including the development and integration of advanced practice roles.  To support this process, officials are collaborating with Professor Juliette Murray, Consultant Breast Surgeon and National Clinical Director at the Centre for Sustainable Delivery, NHS Scotland. Professor Murray brings valuable expertise, having successfully led the redesign of breast services in Scotland in response to similar challenges around demand and capacity.My Department remains committed to collaborating with Trusts and stakeholders to implement sustainable solutions in the delivery of timely, high-quality care for all patients accessing breast services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reasons why there is no control and restraints expert on the panel in Maghaberry Prison, to assess whether a case should be referred to Professional Standards.
answer_text: Maghaberry Prison has a robust process in place to assess when a case involving Control and Restraint should be referred to the Professional Standards Unit.  This process includes a Control and Restraint Instructor, who reviews all concerns regarding excessive Use of Force.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail how much longer is it anticipated the scaffolding at Parliament Buildings will be required to remain in situ.
answer_text: The works will be carried out on a phased basis, with Roof 1 and Courtyard 3 completed first, followed by Courtyards 1 and 2.  Scaffolding will be removed from each area as soon as the works there are finished.  These works are anticipated to be completed by 20 July 2026.  It should be noted that these works constitute Phase 1 of the roof repairs and Phase 2 will carry out the remainder of the remedial works required.  Indicative dates for Phase 2 are currently unknown.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the total cost of the scaffolding at Parliament Buildings since it was first erected.
answer_text: Costs incurred for scaffolding up to 31 October 2025 are £214,847.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the current monthly cost for the hire of scaffolding at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The monthly cost for the hire of the scaffolding is £9,177.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the reason for the scaffolding in place at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The scaffolding was erected in August 2024 to Roof 1, at the rear of Parliament Buildings, and three internal courtyards as these areas were deemed to be the most critical for access.  The areas were scaffolded to mitigate the risk of falling debris, allowing safe access to the slope entrance at the rear of the building, the boiler room and kitchen.  This has allowed for essential planned preventative maintenance within the courtyards to continue. It also allowed access for the Integrated Design Team, ICT, to carry out investigatory works to enable full scoping of the roof repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of an old building, that must be in place to deem it as substantially intact, to qualify for a replacement dwelling, under Planning Policy HOU 20.
answer_text: The Derry City & Strabane District Council Plan Strategy (adopted July 2025) Policy HOU 20: Restored and Replacement Rural Dwellings reflects the regional strategic planning policy for replacement dwellings set out in my Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) in that “Planning permission will be granted for a replacement dwelling where the building to be replaced exhibits the essential characteristics of a dwelling and as a minimum, all external structural walls are substantially intact.” Neither policy defines ‘substantially intact’ or specifies the percentage of a building that must be intact for the purposes of determining planning applications for replacement dwellings. The vast majority of applications for development in the countryside, including those for replacement dwellings, are determined by the local council planning authority. Applications are assessed on a case-by-case basis, taking account of the local development plan (LDP); regional strategic planning policy (including the SPPS, and PPS 21: Sustainable Development in the Countryside as retained under the transitional arrangements of the SPPS); local circumstances; site characteristics; and all other material considerations. The relevance and weight to be attached to planning policy and all other material considerations is a matter of professional planning judgement for the relevant planning authority as decision taker, in any individual case. It is also important to note that the application of planning policy is continuously being refined through operational experience, the courts and at planning appeals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Tullywhisker Primary School, Strabane, had a NI Water connection, prior to its closure in June 2002.
answer_text: Connections to NI Water infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have requested this information. NI Water has advised that a review of its mapping system has identified that there was a water connection at the school.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the number of small businesses in West Tyrone that have availed of support from Invest NI in the last two years.
answer_text: Over the last 2 years Invest NI has provided 126 offers of support to 72 Micro and Small businesses within the West Tyrone Parliamentary Constituency area totalling £2.6m.  This assistance is expected to leverage a total investment of £11.2 million and, over the next 3 to 5 years, lead to the creation of 110 new jobs in micro and small businesses in the area. The number of jobs supported by Invest NI in micro and small businesses in the West Tyrone area has increased by 96% on the previous two years.88% of the businesses supported over this period were Micro or Small and these businesses accounted for 87% of assistance and 88% of new jobs in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients with a dementia diagnosis who have experienced delayed discharge from hospital in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The data on the number of people with a dementia diagnosis who have experienced a delayed discharge from hospital in the last 12 months is currently not available. The Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system, Encompass. As the Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, the data generated within the context of service delivery is subject to a process of extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to submit for reporting purposes.I acknowledge that there is a significant issue in regards to securing placements for people with dementia from hospital. This is an issue that is currently being considered by the Provider Collaborative Forum for Enhanced Care. The Forum is testing a concept of offering an enhanced care rate to the independent care home sector for dementia placements. The intention of this exercise is to create additional capacity in the independent care home sector, to effect more timely discharge from hospital for patients with dementia and to ease pressure upon hospital beds over this winter period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any financial assessment that has been made of the implications of increasing dementia prevalence for Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how this will be reflected in future multiyear budget planning.
answer_text: The projected population increase in older people and people living with Dementia undoubtedly places additional demands on the provision of health and social care services. My Department is giving particular attention to Dementia services through the work of the Regional Dementia Project Board. This has been established to oversee regional improvements where these can be delivered and recognising the significantly challenging financial position and competing budgetary pressures.While work has commenced on a multi-year budget exercise it is not possible to provide a definitive assessment of the Department’s future funding position for Dementia until final Budget settlements have been agreed by the Executive.  At this point the process is at an early stage, and it will be some time before budget outcomes are confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reasons for the delay in commissioning the two theatres in the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: My department is actively working to commission all available theatre capacity in the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), however it will take time and recurrent funding to build the HSC core capacity. In the interim period, we are working in partnership with the independent sector to ensure that SWAH facilities are utilised. The work to establish this and other contracts started in May this year, following the publication of the Elective Care Framework: Implementation and Funding Plan, and we continue to work with a range of independent sector providers to secure additional capacity. The newly commissioned theatre capacity will be used to help reduce the backlog of NHS patients who are currently waiting for primary knee or primary hip procedures and we continue to explore other opportunities to increase the range of procedures which can be undertaken on this site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made to date on recruiting staff required to restore emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust have advised that the temporary change to Emergency General Surgery in South West Acute Hospital remains in place. The Trust has recruited to Trust wide Consultant Surgeon posts and currently there are no locum agency doctors at Consultant level.My Department remains very mindful of the challenges which all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland face in both maintaining and reorganising services, not least in our smaller hospitals. The Trust, supported by the DoH, remains fully committed to the long-term development and vision for SWAH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he plans to have the two theatres at the South West Acute Hospital which are not yet in use, commissioned and fully utilized.
answer_text: My department is actively working to commission all available theatre capacity in the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), however it will take time and recurrent funding to build the HSC core capacity. In the interim period, we are working in partnership with the independent sector to ensure that SWAH facilities are utilised. The work to establish this and other contracts started in May this year, following the publication of the Elective Care Framework: Implementation and Funding Plan, and we continue to work with a range of independent sector providers to secure additional capacity. The newly commissioned theatre capacity will be used to help reduce the backlog of NHS patients who are currently waiting for primary knee or primary hip procedures and we continue to explore other opportunities to increase the range of procedures which can be undertaken on this site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to increase services at the (i) South West Acute Hospital; and (ii) Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex.
answer_text: My department is actively working to commission all available theatre capacity in the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), however it will take time and recurrent funding to build the HSC core capacity. In the interim period, we are working in partnership with the independent sector to ensure that SWAH facilities are utilised. The work to establish this and other contracts started in May this year, following the publication of the Elective Care Framework: Implementation and Funding Plan, and we continue to work with a range of independent sector providers to secure additional capacity. The newly commissioned theatre capacity will be used to help reduce the backlog of NHS patients who are currently waiting for primary knee or primary hip procedures and we continue to explore other opportunities to increase the range of procedures which can be undertaken on this site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to make the online booking of GP appointments mandatory across all practices.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce. At this point in time it is not mandatory for GP practices to make online appointments available to their patients.  While all practices have the technology available to enable this, it remains their choice in how they manage and deliver their services within their individual practice.  My Department has recently issued guidance to practices relating to the management of patient demand and optimising workflow to maximise efficiency, and consideration of how technology can be best used to improve experience for both patients and practices. This best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, includes recommendations that Practices should maximise the use of digital platforms to meet the growing demand for remote services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail, when an employee of the Civil Service requests details of an alleged misconduct case against them, from their Line Manager, whether that information should be provided.
answer_text: ANSWEREach case is assessed on an individual basis as permitted for within the Disciplinary policy. For minor disciplinary offences the line manager should provide any evidence that has come to light and give the employee the opportunity to explain their actions or clarify the facts of the case. Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that an employee has committed an act of misconduct, they will be invited to attend a disciplinary hearing. They will be invited to this meeting in writing, and this letter will set out the charge(s) against them and the details of any supporting facts. This letter is issued by NICS HR.

answer_text: ANSWERNICSHR L&D are responsible for corporate induction which is aimed at staff completely new to the Civil Service, delivered through both Face-to-Face and e-Learning providing a corporate overview of the NICS. Each department is responsible for their own Departmental and local inductions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail, when an employee of the Civil Service is notified that they are under investigation for alleged misconduct, at what stage are they informed of the nature of that alleged misconduct.
answer_text: ANSWERStaff who have alleged misconduct case against them will be invited to attend a disciplinary hearing. They are invited to this meeting in writing, and this letter sets out the allegation(s) against them and the details of any supporting facts, as is outlined in the NICS Discipline policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to ensure that disciplinary investigations in the Civil Service are of an independent nature, and fully adhere to Civil Service policies and procedures.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service use HR Connect to carry out independent disciplinary investigations. These investigations are set up to adhere to Civil Service policies and procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any safeguards being put in place to protect teaching staff from students putting photographs or recordings of them, on social media sites.
answer_text: Boards of Governors and school leaders have a central role in ensuring effective safeguarding practices within the school, including the protection and wellbeing of staff. Schools are encouraged to make pupils aware, through safeguarding and online safety education, of the serious implications of such behaviour. The unauthorised taking or sharing of photographs, or recording of staff by pupils, particularly when posted on social media, may constitute harassment or a breach of privacy. Where this occurs, the matter should be reported to the school principal and managed in line with the school’s behaviour policies. The department continues to support schools through guidance on online safety and safeguarding frameworks, which reinforce the duty to protect both pupils and staff, with additional advice and support available through Education Authority support services in line with departmental policy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action he is taking to streamline the current planning system, given that it will have a detrimental impact on the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: I welcome the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill and wish to ensure that the Bill aligns with existing roads and planning legislation – particularly where derelict buildings may pose a risk to the public road network or impact local development. Under section 23 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011, and Class A, Part 33 of The Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (NI) 2015, I am content that the planning system will not be deleterious to actions arising from the outworking of the Dilapidation Bill (when enacted) particularly with regard to the carrying out of works for the maintenance, improvement or other alteration of any building, including demolition.You will be aware of the ongoing Planning Improvement Programme (PIP) which aims to improve the efficiencey and effectiveness of the planning system.  One of the actions within PIP is the pilot with Belfast City Council to review the need for demolition consent application (DCA) notifications to the Department.  The pilot aims to streamline the notification process while ensuring appropriate oversight of heritage impacts.  My officials are currently reviewing the outcome of the pilot and will update me in due course.I do not believe it necessary to consider further measures to streamline the planning system specific to this Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the impact of the climate change targets on any future capital builds, within our school estates.
answer_text: I am aware that climate change targets will no doubt have an impact on future capital builds within our school estates, however at this time I am not in a position to advise on the extent or cost implications of future targets, nor has capital been allocated to my Department to address climate change measures.However, in advance of emerging requirements the Department is committed to ensuring all new build schools announced from 2022 achieve Near Zero Energy Building status. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the development of the draft Children’s and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.
answer_text: The Department of Health is consulting on the Health and Social Care Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing (EHWB) Framework. The Framework, when finalised, will support health and social care services and practitioners to collaborate with other services, agencies and key stakeholders in order to rationalise, coordinate and integrate high-quality, evidence-based support, intervention and diagnostic care pathways that best meet the needs of children, young people and their families.Public consultation and online events were held in August and September 2025, as well as opportunity to provide consultation responses.  Consultations were also held with key statutory and professional bodies, as well as the Mental Health Champion and the Autism Reviewer.The Public Consultation ran from 22nd May to 26th September 2025. Data analysis and report preparation are scheduled for October 2025 and November 2025, with the final report expected to be available for review by 28th November 2025.The Public Consultation ran from 22nd May to 26th September 2025. Data analysis and report preparation are scheduled for October 2025 and November 2025, with the final report expected to be available for review by 28th November 2025. The findings presented in the report will inform the final version of the Framework and guide the development of an implementation plan. Work on the final Framework will commence following the report’s publication. My Department remains on track to achieve the goal of publishing the final version of the Framework by June 2026.
