question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there any plans to introduce an online appointment booking system for GP practices via a mobile phone app.
answer_text: As part of the Northern Ireland General Practice Digital Care Services (NIGPDCS) framework contract, established in April 2024, and its predecessor contract the Northern Ireland General Practice System of Choice Framework (NIGPSOC) contract my Department continues to fund the provision of both an online GP appointment booking service and a Repeat medication ordering service.  These services have been in place since April 2016 and both services are accessible via a mobile app.  Although these services are available to all GP practices, as independent contractors not all GP practices choose to make appointments or the online repeat medication ordering service available to their patients. Whilst the decision to make appointments and/or the repeat medication ordering service available online is made at an individual GP practice level, my Department as part of the GMS contract arrangements requires all GP practices to promote the use of online services with their patients

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with GPs in order to address the ongoing issue for patients in having to make numerous telephone calls before getting a connection when trying to book an appointment.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce. In recent years the Department has made significant investment to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems and employ additional staff to manage their systems efficiently.As part of the GMS contract for 25/26 my Department has introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services. These measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels.  Rather, they aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike.  In many cases, these are things that practices here are already doing and the aim has been to seek to spread that good practice across the region.In addition, my Department established a GP Access Working Group to explore issues relating to access to services and develop a programme of work to help address these and improve the experience for patients. The Working Group has overseen the development of guidance for practices relating to the management of patient demand and optimising workflow to maximise efficiency, and consideration of how technology can be best used to improve experience for both patients and practices. This best practice guide, ‘Making all Contacts Count’, has now been finalised and disseminated to all GP Practices.  My Department is also taking forward work to develop a new model of neighbourhood care that will enable providers to work more effectively together, that delivers more care in primary and community settings in order to enable people to stay healthier for longer, reducing the demand for expensive, hospital-led care. This work will proceed at pace in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has an obligation to control noxious weeds on lands, which were vested for the new A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, to curb the spread onto lands currently being farmed.
answer_text: Following the A5WTC High Court judgment delivered on Monday 23 June 2025, The Judge ruled that the Orders including the Vesting Orders (VO) would be quashed. The Court Order was filed on 27 June 2025, and ownership of vested lands, by law, reverted to the original landowners with effect from that date. As such, my Department do not have an obligation to control noxious weeds on these lands at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department received permission from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, prior to the cutting of trees and the removal of hedgerows, on all lands vested for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The trees and hedgerows that were removed as part of the Advance Works on the A5 project were all within the lands vested to facilitate construction of the scheme. As such, the necessary works were included within the Environmental Statement and associated addenda for the project and no specific permission was therefore required from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs regarding their removal.  On site, the works were completed by the Contractor in line with the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985 (as amended by The Wildlife and Natural Environment Act (NI) 2011) and in collaboration with the Ecological Clerk of Works to ensure this legislation was adhered to.Licences were sought from DAERA prior to felling of trees where there was evidence of potential bat activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31223/22-27, to detail (i) which three hospitals in the Western Health and Social Care Trust have social work staff shortages; and (ii) the number of (a) full-time vacancies; and (b) part-time vacancies, in each hospital.
answer_text: There are currently social work staff shortages in Altnagelvin, South West Acute and Omagh Hospitals and no vacancies in Waterside Hospital. 	Altnagelvin Hospital has funding for 11 whole time equivalent (WTE) social workers and at present there are 2 WTE (37.5 hours per week each) short-term sickness absences. The Trust are able to recruit on a temporary basis into one of these positions, however a recent recruitment process was unsuccessful and will be undertaken again.   South West Acute Hospital has funding for 4 WTE social workers and at present have 2 WTE permanent vacant positions and they are also recruiting into a 1 WTE temporary vacant position.  These posts have been advertised, however, unfortunately the recruitment process was unsuccessful in filling any of these vacancies and the positions will be re-advertised.  Omagh Hospital has funding for 2 WTE Social Workers and currently both are vacant due to 1 WTE sickness absence and 1 WTE secondment to another role.  The Western Health and Social Care Trust Hospital Social Work Team are continuing to advertise the vacancies, both permanent and temporary, in order to backfill the positions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any direct engagement his Department has had with farmers affected by the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to supporting those impacted by the A5 Western Transport Corridor project. Responsibility for the vesting and restoration of lands is a matter solely for the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and as such, direct engagement with farmers affected by the A5 continues to be undertaken by DfI. My Department has provided input to DfI letters to advise how the A5 vesting would affect claims for farm subsidies. The advice also included signposting of queries to DAERA at our DAERA Direct Offices, Area based Schemes and Countryside Management Branches. This assistance was made available to all impacted farm businesses, and I can confirm that 35 farm businesses availed of face-to-face support with their 2025 claim for farm subsidy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assistance his Department has provided for farm businesses affected by A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to supporting those impacted by the A5 Western Transport Corridor project, however, responsibility for the vesting and restoration of lands is a matter solely for the Department for Infrastructure (DfI).  DfI confirmed that landowners affected by the A5 would not have access to vested land for the 2025 scheme year. DfI wrote to impacted landowners, including input from my officials advising how this would affect claims for farm subsidies. The letters included signposting of queries to DAERA at our DAERA Direct Offices, Area based Schemes and Countryside Management Branches. This assistance was made available to all impacted farm businesses, and I can confirm that 35 farm businesses availed of face-to-face support with their 2025 claim for farm subsidy. Following the judgment on the A5 and during this period of uncertainty I approved the decision that penalties would not be applied to land included in the vesting process. This decision was communicated to the AERA Committee on 23 June 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what information officials from the Department for Infrastructure have requested from his Department relating to farm businesses affected by the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to working collaboratively with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) in working through the implications of A5 vesting on impacted farm businesses. Responsibility for the vesting and restoration of lands is a matter solely for the DfI. On confirmation from DfI that landowners affected by the A5 would not have access to vested land for the 2025 scheme year, my officials provided input to a DfI letter, advising how this would affect claims for farm subsidies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of farm businesses, affected by the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, that have opted for (i) the rental offer for the land affected; and (ii) the restoration of the land affected.
answer_text: There are 309 landowners covered by the A5 WTC Vesting Orders which have now been quashed, the majority of whom are impacted by the Advance Works that have been carried out. Discussions with these landowners are at an early stage and to date, 22 landowners have expressed an interest in entering into a Licence Agreement and 53 have indicated a preference for reinstatement works.  Some landowners have not yet expressed a preference, and a number are undecided. Officials are actively engaging with landowners and their agents and these numbers will change as further meetings are held, details are discussed, and final decisions are made by the landowners in the course of the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans she has to improve safety measures on sections of the existing A5 carriageway.
answer_text: At the outset I will repeat that my ultimate aim is to ensure the delivery of the A5 Flagship project, and I am determined to do all I can to ensure this happens so that no more lives are lost on this road.The most important point made by the court was the need for a new and safer A5 dual carriageway, so that no other name is added to the long list of those who have died on the existing road. That is my focus.Last financial year, my Department undertook a series of targeted road safety enhancements along the existing A5 corridor, aimed at improving visibility, driver awareness, and surface performance. These works included refreshing white lines, replacing signs, cats eyes and completing localised resurfacing.  My Department has programmed further resurfacing work this financial year. A report highlighting additional potential safety improvements on the A5 is being prepared by officials.  I will be giving consideration to these proposals in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant of AQW 30296/22-27, what is the timeframe for the (i) scoping work; and (ii) the development of a risk based plan.
answer_text: As advised in my response to AQW 30296/22-27, my department is progressing the work to identify the resources needed to take this significant task forward, which will include a scoping exercise, developing the risk-based plan and implementation of the plan. Delivery timescales will depend on available resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he plans to address the shortfall of funding in mental health services.
answer_text: It is widely recognised that mental health services in Northern Ireland have historically been underfunded in comparison with other UK jurisdictions. Our mental health services require additional funding to meet current and future needs. Within this context, the Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 31 and its accompanying Funding Plan, published in June 2021, were intended to serve as a 10-year roadmap to promote good mental health and improve mental health services. The Funding Plan recognised that implementation of the Strategy’s 35 actions would require a significant investment, estimated at £1.2 billion over ten years (based on 2021 costs).  However, to date, no additional funding has been provided to my Department to support implementation of the Strategy and so existing Departmental resources have been used to progress many of the actions.  A total of £12.3 million, has been utilised across 20 specific Strategy actions from 2021 to the end of 2024/25. This represents only 16% of the funding identified (£76.93m) as required for the Mental Health Strategy by the end of 2024/25.The absence of dedicated additional funding has necessitated a partial and phased implementation and, undoubtably, progress has been constrained in areas due to the challenging financial position.  Mental health remains a priority for me and the Mental Health Strategy is a key vehicle for bringing about changes to mental health services. However, in the absence of additional funding being allocated to my Department for the implementation of the Strategy, future funding decisions will inevitably reflect the ongoing financial constraints facing the Health and Social Care system.

This represents only 16% of the funding identified (£76.93m) as required for the Mental Health Strategy by the end of 2024/25.The absence of dedicated additional funding has necessitated a partial and phased implementation and, undoubtably, progress has been constrained in areas due to the challenging financial position.  In the absence of additional funding being allocated to my Department for the implementation of the Strategy, future funding decisions will inevitably reflect the ongoing financial constraints facing the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why there has been a delay within the Education Authority in finalising the acquisition of the Strabane Grammar School site on Liskey Road, Strabane given that all necessary documentation are now in place.
answer_text: The disposal of the site was paused in late 2023 at the local Council’s request, to allow the Council to progress completion of the PAD process and to obtain the Land & Property Services (LPS) valuation.Now that these steps have been completed and confirmed, the Education Authority (EA) will instruct LPS to recommence the disposal process whilst the Council and Clanmail seek their necessary approvals.The EA is striving to complete the disposal and all related legalities as quickly as possible, likely early in the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Acute Mental Health facility to be developed as the third phase of the Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex.
answer_text: The outline business case for the proposed acute mental health facility at Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex is currently being revised by the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) to reflect changes in mental health staffing arrangements and other strategic needs, including analysis of population health needs. Department of Health officials are in contact with the WHSCT regarding the revised business case.Following the announcement of the outcome of the 2025 UK wide Spending Review in June, the Department of Finance commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. Whilst I remain hopeful, my ability to commit to funding capital projects, including the Omagh Mental Health Unit, will depend on the Department of Health capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what he is doing to address the shortage of oxygen for those suffering from cystic fibrosis who depend on oxygen.
answer_text: A procured Oxygen Service is currently provided by BOC Homecare Service for patients who require home treatment with oxygen in Northern Ireland. HSC Trusts and GPs access this same service. 				To ensure there are adequate supplies and to ensure the correct patients get oxygen therapy in line with their needs there is an agreed prioritisation process with Trust respiratory leads.				SPPG, together with colleagues from each of the 5 Trust areas, meet with BOC on a bi-monthly basis to discuss the contract performance in relation to the KPIs specified within the procured oxygen contract.  		SPPG have contacted BOC in response to your query and I can confirm there are no current shortages in oxygen or delays in delivery of home oxygen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of appointments cancelled by the Western Health and Social Care Trust Waiting Times Office in (i) the South West Acute Hospital; (ii) Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex; and (iii) Altnagelvin Area Hospital, in the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he has put in place to reduce waiting times for patients red-flagged for cancer assessments.
answer_text: I acknowledge that current red flag waiting times for cancer assessment, including outpatient assessment and diagnostics, is unacceptable. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.Building elective services that are fit for purpose in the long term requires us to do things better, smarter and in a way that is sustainable. The Department of Health’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) and Implementation Plan sets out the strategic direction over the next five years to ensure a sustainable, equitable and efficient system.  The size of the challenge in addressing waiting lists or the reform and capacity building needed in the long term cannot be underestimated.  For elective care, this means reforming services by adopting a whole system approach to meet the population and system needs and the ECF is the mechanism which will transform this into practice.Through this work my Department will aim to deliver services differently, through service reviews and regional configuration to better align demand and capacity. Our focus will ensure that we deliver the right treatment, in the right place, at the right time for our patients. This will include enhancing opportunities to work across the primary and secondary care interface to ensure that patients are being managed in the most appropriate setting. Our rolling programme of Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) reviews and service reviews, alongside our work with clinical networks and other regional groups, will identify opportunities for regional transformation, standardise and streamline service delivery and new pathways, and reduce unwanted clinical variation across the region. Reducing waiting times is one of the most pressing challenges facing the Health and Social Care Sector and the Northern Ireland Executive.  £85m has been identified recurrently this year to build red flag/time-critical capacity. While this capacity is being developed non-recurrent funding has also been provided to support the reduction of red flag and time critical waiting lists. To date (April to September) £38m has been allocated non-recurrently for red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments. This funding has been used to secure additional capacity, both in-house and in the independent sector, across a range of elective specialties.Steps are also being taken to improve the appropriateness and quality of suspect cancer referrals. This includes ongoing updates to referral guidelines to ensure they reflect current clinical evidence, alongside targeted education and information sessions for GPs to support accurate and timely decision-making in respect of referring patients for cancer assessment in secondary care.Two Rapid Diagnostic Centres at Whiteabbey and South Tyrone Hospitals have been introduced which will

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether there is a shortage of hospital social workers in the Western Health and Social Care Trust area, and (ii) the impact this is having on delayed discharges, particularly where patients ready to leave hospital are placed on a waiting list for assessment.
answer_text: At present there are social work staff shortages in 3 of the 4 hospitals within the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT).  There are 24 whole time equivalent (WTE) funded social work positions within the WHSCT hospital social work service.   At present there are 6 WTE vacancies (25%).  2 of these vacancies are permanent and 4 are temporary positions.  The WHSCT are actively recruiting into these positions to fill both the temporary and permanent vacancies. Staffing vacancies will result in a delay in service provision.  However, at present the WHSCT hospital social work team are prioritising urgent referrals and those individuals ready for discharge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to restore emergency general surgery in the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Following the pause in the consultation on Emergency General Surgery in the Western Trust, I have asked the Trust to use this immediate time to restore confidence by producing a vision plan emphasising how the South West Acute Hospital will be supported to meet both the needs of its current and future population.  The Western Trust’s Board will meet on 2 October 20205, to make decisions on the current paused consultation, and the work ahead on a “Vision” for SWAH.  The Trust will work closely with staff, community leaders, elected representatives, the public and officials from my Department as they prepare the Vision.  My Department remains very mindful of the challenges which all Trusts in Northern Ireland face in both maintaining and reorganising services, not least in our smaller hospitals. The Trust, supported by the DoH, remains fully committed to the long-term development and vision for SWAH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there is a specialist paediatrician to diagnose Paediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorder Associated with Streptococcal Infections; and (ii) where they are based.
answer_text: I recognise that this rare condition causes distress and concern for children and their families. Paediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorder Associated with Group A Streptococcus (PANDAS) and Paediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS) are related disorders that present with the acute onset of multiple somatic and neuropsychiatric symptoms. PANS and PANDAS are considered post-infectious autoimmune and/or neuroinflammatory disorders.When a child presents with symptoms, they are typically seen at their local District General Hospital by a general paediatrician rather than a specialist. The general paediatrician will assess the child, as this disorder usually involves a range of symptoms. Care may be provided at any of the District General Hospitals in Northern Ireland, where there is an acute paediatric team and access to local Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS).The paediatrician will review each case and, based on the symptoms, may request additional support from the Regional Paediatric Neurology service or the Regional Paediatric Immunology service at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC), among other specialties, to develop a treatment plan.Given the potential neuropsychiatric implications of this disorder, it is essential to engage with CAMHS colleagues in Trusts, as they play a crucial role in the care and treatment of affected children.Currently, there is no NICE guidance for this condition and limited national clinical guidance. Treatment and management focus on addressing symptoms, which are predominantly neuropsychiatric. There are no specialist paediatricians dedicated to diagnosing this condition; diagnosis is primarily made by excluding other conditions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any action they have taken in relation to concerns expressed by many communities around the levels of immigration in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will introduce tuition fees support for Northern Ireland students undertaking a Graduate Entry Medicine course in a UK university.
answer_text: My predecessor was pleased to introduce loans for local students to cover the costs of tuition at the Graduate Entry Medical School at Ulster University’s Magee campus. This will attract more students to Derry, supporting the Executive’s Programme for Government commitment, and produce much-needed medical professionals for our health service.While I have no current plans to introduce tuition fee support for local students undertaking Graduate Entry Medicine courses at universities in Britain, I have recently endorsed the draft Terms of Reference for a Review of Higher Education Funding. As part of the review, an assessment of all student finance support arrangements will be carried out. The Department of Health has responsibility for policy in respect of those studying to enter healthcare professions and my officials will be working closely with their counterparts to ensure that our student support package supports the widest possible access to higher education, and in particular courses such as medicine, while balancing affordability for students and the public purse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps are being taken by his Department to control nutrient nitrate and phosphorus concentrations from single dwellings discharging into river catchments in unfavourable condition.
answer_text: My department holds approximately 150,000 records, spanning five decades, relating to single dwellings with private treatment systems. These discharge consents typically do not set limits for specific chemicals like nitrates and phosphorus, given the low volumes and that most treatment methods focus on removing biological contaminants and reducing public health impacts.For single dwellings, and discharges below 5 cubic metres per day, reported issues are investigated, and any non-compliance is considered in accordance with the DAERA Enforcement Policy.My department has commenced recruitment for two new staff to gather and analyse data on discharges from point sources including, single dwellings, starting with the Lough Neagh catchment and later expanding to other affected areas. This aims to improve understanding and enable more effective water quality interventions.In line with the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan, my department now have a high-level Water Quality Communications Strategy in place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in order to address nutrient nitrate and phosphorus concentrations and water quality thresholds, what existing Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 consents have been reviewed by his Department since July 2023 for discharges into river catchments in unfavourable condition.
answer_text: My department recognise that there is a requirement to undertake a review of consents, and other authorisations, in respect of sites designated for their conservation importance, including those in unfavourable condition. Work to scope resource requirements to develop a risk-based plan and methodologies for reviewing all 150,000 consents and other authorisations that impact water quality is currently being undertaken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in order to address nutrient nitrate and phosphorus concentrations and water quality thresholds since July 2023, what steps are being taken by his Department to review existing Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 consents for discharges into river catchments in unfavourable condition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what schemes are in place to control nutrient nitrate and phosphorus concentrations from diffuse agricultural sources into river catchments in unfavourable condition.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a range of measures to improve the efficient use of nutrients and control losses to waterways. These include the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, which provides farmers across Northern Ireland with information and training to help them optimise the use of nutrients, such as phosphorus, to reduce excess application and run-off to watercourses.  Another measure is Phase 2 of the Small Business Research Initiative on the Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry.  This project aims to establish centralised demonstrator projects for processing of livestock slurry and anaerobic digestate to reduce surplus phosphorus within NI agriculture and ensure efficient export and recycling of organic nutrients.The Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP) is currently operating in five river catchments and aims to reduce agricultural pressures on water quality in these areas.  This SCP provides grant support and invites farmers to work with Rivers Trust advisors to ensure that the right measures are implemented in the right places on each individual farm to maximise both water quality and farm benefits.  The expansion of the SCP into other catchments is currently being scoped as an action under the Lough Neagh Action Plan.  The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations are the primary mechanism to control nutrient losses to water from agricultural sources in Northern Ireland. The NAP includes measures on the storage, management and spreading of organic manures and chemical fertilisers and is currently undergoing a fourth periodic review to ensure it remains effective and complies with statutory obligations.  In addition to the NAP, the Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) Regulations require large poultry and pig farms to meet a range of environmental requirements, including the completion of a nutrient management plan.  My Department launched the first phase of the Farming with Nature Package, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, in June 2025, which aims to increase the extent of habitats on agricultural land by, for example, protecting watercourses with riparian buffer strips.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who made the decision to press on with the enabling works on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme while a High Court case was looming; and (ii) how will they be held to account for such a decision.
answer_text: Having considered the various options available and the associated costs and risks, my predecessor, Minister O’Dowd, agreed to progress with certain advance works as the best way forward for the A5 WTC project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the compensation her Department is making available for farmers on the route of A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, who have had their land vested and left in an unrecognisable state.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders, taking effect from the 27 June 2025.  At that time the lands returned to the ownership of the landowners from whom they were vested.  The Department has written to all landowners setting out how it plans to manage the process of returning the vested lands following the quashing of the orders.The Department has also met all the known land agents and the UFU to assist the landowners in this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she expects to have a response to her Department’s appeal to the High Court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: I wrote to Executive colleagues on 11 July 2025 to provide an update on the judgement and to clarify that, as Minister for Infrastructure, the decision to appeal rests with me. Given the project’s flagship status and the Executive’s unanimous support in October 2024, I have invited their views before reaching a final decision. I remain committed to working constructively and collaboratively with colleagues to secure a solution that delivers the road and saves lives. Any appeal must be lodged within six weeks of the judgement taking effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her rationale for making a statement to the media on her proposal to appeal the High Court ruling on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, rather than first coming to the floor of the Assembly and informing the elected Members.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of keeping elected Members informed on significant matters. In this instance, I made a public statement regarding my  intentionto appeal the High Court ruling on the A5 scheme to ensure timely and transparent communication with the public, given the level of interest in the project. I then engaged with my Executive colleagues to gather their views, before I made my decision.The Department’s Appeal has now been served, and it would be inappropriate for me to comment further on the detail of the Department’s case given court proceedings are ongoing.This does not diminish my commitment to engaging with the Assembly, and I will continue to do so through the appropriate channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, now that the land vested for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme has been handed back to the land owners, to outline what steps her Department is taking to have the fencing removed and the land restored to its former state.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders on the 27 June 2025. The Department will work with all landowners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the outcomes from her Department’s recent consultations on off-grid heating and the decarbonisation of rural energy systems.
answer_text: My Department has recently held a consultation on low carbon heat support and a call for evidence on the potential use of biofuels to support the energy transition in off-gas grid properties. Engagement has been highly positive, with strong participation from industry stakeholders exploring a range of issues, including energy efficiency, economic considerations, and supply chain viability. Officials are currently analysing the responses provided and my Department plans to publish a government response on low carbon heat support in the coming weeks, with the response on biofuels following later this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that approximately 76 per cent of households are connected to the gas grid and around 68 per cent rely on heating oil, to outline what steps her Department is taking to ensure these communities are not left behind in the energy transition to net zero.
answer_text: The latest 2021 census figures show there are 768,810 occupied households, of which 256,500 (approx. 33.4%) are connected to the gas grid for their main heating system.  480,600 (approx. 62.5%) indicated that oil was their primary heating fuel.The Executive’s Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero, sets the long-term direction to reach net zero carbon by 2050, with a key principle of replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy.In keeping with this principle, my Department is exploring options to offer support for low carbon heating technologies following a consultation that closed earlier this year. We have also published a Call for Evidence for both liquid biofuels and biomethane, as we continue to explore their respective roles in the energy transition. My department continues to work in collaboration across government and with stakeholders to ensure that the transition to net zero is fair, inclusive, and sustainable for all energy users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will commit to a technology-neutral approach that includes support for drop-in fuels like BioLPG, which offer lower upfront costs and minimal disruption, compared to heat pumps in future energy policy for household heating systems.
answer_text: Electrification is a foundational component of the decarbonisation of household heating; it offers significant efficiency savings over the continued use of fossil fuels whilst making use of our abundant renewable electricity resources. In certain circumstances, drop-in fuels could have the potential to offer practical benefits during the energy transition. Policies in this area will need to consider many factors including upfront and ongoing running costs of potential solutions. My Department is progressing work to ensure a holistic approach is taken to heat policy, we have recently consulted on offering support for low carbon heating technologies such as heat pumps and have also published a Call for Evidence for both liquid biofuels and biomethane, as we continue to explore their respective roles in the energy transition. My department remains committed to working in collaboration across government and with key stakeholders to ensure that the transition to net zero is fair, inclusive, and sustainable for all energy users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) discussions; (ii) meetings; and (iii) correspondence her Department has had with (a) Fermanagh and Omagh District Council (FODC); (b) Geological Survey of Northern Ireland (GSNI); and (c) jointly with FODC and GSNI, in relation to mineral safeguarding, which is to form part of the Fermanagh and Omagh Local Development Plan, as per her Department's Minerals Information Paper.
answer_text: My officials have actively engaged with all district councils throughout the Local Development Plan (LDP) process. This engagement has covered all mineral policy considerations including mineral safeguarding. In March 2017, GSNI published a Minerals Information Paper which aimed to provide councils with a greater understanding of the many factors that need to be considered when developing a minerals policy for a LDP.There has been engagement between my officials, and the staff of GSNI, and FODC, at both official and representative level, throughout their LDP formulation. It included a workshop in February 2017 which was followed by the production of a further paper by GSNI illustrating the impact of surface infrastructure on the availability of mineral resources. Furthermore, following a request from FODC in December 2019, information on areas of gold resource safeguarding within the local area district was provided by GSNI.My officials, and colleagues in GSNI, will continue to provide any help and assistance required by Councils in the development and monitoring of their LDPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what support his Department will provide to the farm businesses who had their land vested for the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme to assist them in having it reinstated for farming purposes.

I am aware that DfI colleagues will be writing to the affected landowners shortly to agree a way forward. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will apply equivalent nutrient (nitrate and phosphorus) concentrations and water quality thresholds, as applied for the water discharge consent for the Dalradian goldmine at Curraghinalt, to agriculture, wastewater treatment plants and housing in all unfavourable river catchments.
answer_text: NIEA assesses all applications for consent to discharge on their own merits and undertake an appropriate technical assessment to set suitable discharge limits. These limits are based on the nature and volume of the discharge and the status of the receiving water body, to ensure relevant water quality and ecological objectives can be met. The same approach to setting standards, on which NIEA has invited the Public Local Inquiry on the Dalradian gold applications to give its recommendations, will be applied to all new consents under NIEA’s regulatory control which discharge to locations recorded as being in unfavourable condition or otherwise. NIEA continue to review its consenting methods to ensure appropriate measures are in place to protect the environment. Discharges from single dwellings are not subject to specific conditions for nitrate and phosphorusIt should be noted that NIEA regulate point source discharges under the Water (NI) Order 1999 via consents to discharge, but this framework does not address diffuse pollution from agricultural sources. Inputs to water courses from agricultural activities are subject to regulation under other schemes and controls.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

A high proportion of posts within the Department cannot facilitate hybrid working due to the nature of the roles including, for example, operational posts within the Northern Ireland Prison Service and the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service.Staff within the Department seeking to avail of hybrid working complete a work style agreement (setting out the agreed split between workplace, home or remote working) for approval by their line manager. To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of homeowners along the new A5 Western Transport Corridor that will be protected from noise by mitigating measures such as raised banking and wooden panelling.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to noise mitigation on the A5 Western Transport Corridor has been to integrate mitigation measures as part of the scheme design, keeping the route alignment low within the landscape and in cutting (which provides noise screening) wherever possible, and within the balanced design approach accounting for other constraints and applicable design parameters. This approach has sought to maximise noise attenuation and minimise noise impact along the full length of the scheme. Noise mitigation is therefore embedded within the scheme design. A low-noise road surface is also planned along the length of the new road, providing a 2 to 3 decibels reduction (compared to a standard surface) for all nearby receptors. In addition to the above measures, acoustic barriers have been incorporated where they would provide an effective amount of attenuation to high noise levels. These are proposed at 3 locations, providing attenuation to approximately 11 residential properties. A total of 75 properties have been identified as eligible for provision of noise insulation measures at individual property level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of homeowners along the new A5 Western Transport Corridor that are deemed eligible to have sound insulation measures carried out on their homes; and (ii) when this will be completed.
answer_text: A total of 75 properties across the length of the A5 project now moving forward to construction have been identified for the incorporation of noise insulation measures. Letters have been issued to the owners of these 75 properties explaining the measures that can be provided. It also invites them to take part in the process going forward, which will include a survey at their individual property.  It is proposed that these noise insulation measures will be provided in advance of the commencement of construction in those areas.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the impact on the environment as a result of the removal of hedgerow, in preparation for the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: The impact of hedge removal has been considered as part of the overall A5 environmental assessment process. The main two environmental topic areas reported in the Environmental Statement that consider the removal of hedgerows are ‘Landscape and Visual’ (which is presented in Chapter 8 of the Environmental Statement Addendum 2022) and ‘Ecology and Nature Conservation’ (in Chapter 9).  From a landscape perspective, removal of hedgerows is considered in terms of the effect on landscape character and on visual receptors such as properties or those passing through the landscape. The incorporation of appropriate embedded landscape mitigation has formed an integral part of the assessment process in seeking to reduce the overall impact of the project. Such measures include the extensive planting of new boundary hedgerows throughout the scheme as part of an overall landscape strategy. From an ecological perspective, the effect on hedgerow removal was assessed both in terms of loss of vegetation and also how wildlife may be affected.The planting scheme takes into account these impacts in developing mitigation measures, for example the provision of tree, scrub, hedgerows and grassland planting helps mitigate for the loss of suitable bat foraging habitat.These mitigation measures are presented in Figure 5.2 (Environmental Mitigation and Landscaping Strategy) of the Environmental Statement Addendum 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are any potential dangers to (i) human health; and (ii) animal health following the pollution incident in Glenmornan River, Artigarvan.
answer_text: Loughs Agency staff discovered dead fish in the Glenmornan River, Artigarvan on the morning of Friday 14 February 2025. Potential sources of pollution have been identified and statutory samples have been collected.  The investigation into the nature of the pollutant involved is ongoing.		DAERA does not provide general advice on human health or health risk matters.  The Public Health Agency (PHA) is the appropriate body to provide such advice.  PHA is contactable through the following link: Contact us | HSC Public Health Agency.  However, further to the recent incident, I can confirm that in relation to human health protection, the Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI) has no record of abstraction points, along the Glenmornan River, which would supply the treated mains water network from Northern Ireland Water.  In addition, the DWI does not have on its register any record of private water supplies being drawn from this surface water.  As such, there are currently no concerns with drinking water quality, in this area, as a result of any recent incidents in the river catchment.		In general, risks to animal health from river water include bacterial infections such as Salmonella, and Leptospirosis, toxins such as blue green algae and possibly other forms of contamination depending on what is discharged into the river.  The risk to animals is dependent on the nature and degree of any contamination from upstream.  Regarding animals using the river for drinking, the Biosecurity Code for Northern Ireland Farms advice is that mains or borehole water of a similar microbiological standard is provided for drinking.  Where there is a visibly polluted river, animals should be provided with water from another potable source.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle which were green-tagged for removal from their herd due to showing signs of bovine TB (bTB) following a skin test have had the green tags removed by a Department vet after a blood test showed no signs of that animal being affected by bTB in the last three years.
answer_text: A green tag is applied by the testing vet to animals that have given a positive reaction to the tuberculin skin test, this allows for the collection of an ear tissue sample for possible DNA testing.  In herds in which there is a suspicion of atypical skin reactions (i.e. skin reactions not typical of the reaction to tuberculin), reactors may undergo blood sampling for Interferon Gamma testing. Following comparison of the skin test results and Interferon Gamma (blood) results, as well as an individual animal veterinary risk assessment, some of the animals may not be taken as bTB reactor animals and remain on farm. These animals remain restricted on farm until they undergo further testing at subsequent herd tests. The default position for these animals is that the tags are not removed although there is no specific reason to retain them once the herd has been derestricted. If the herd owner requests removal, then DAERA permits the tags to be removed under supervision by DAERA staff, usually at the next herd test rather than carrying out a specific visit to remove the tags.Enquires with local Divisional Veterinary Offices indicate that permission was given to remove 31 of these tags in the past 3 years, but no record is kept as to whether or not the tags have actually been removed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: There is an expectation that parties to public local inquiries into planning applications meet their own expenses and that the legislative provisions under which the inquiry will be held make no provision for the public funding of parties. To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what progress has been made on the Victims’ Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of week commencing 24 February 2025, over £80 million has been paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.  	The Victims’ Payments Board has received over 10,803 applications and made 2,604 determinations, with 1,695 of these recommended for a payment.  We recently met with the President of the Victims’ Payments Board to hear directly from him on the progress of the Scheme and the current challenges. We discussed the concerns that have been raised on the length of time it can take to process an application.  We remain assured that the Board is doing all it can to increase the pace and that applications are being dealt with in a trauma-informed way. This is evidenced by the significant year on year increase in the amount paid to victims, with more than £41million paid out to date in the current financial year, compared to just over £23million in 2023/24.  In addition, the number of applications determined in the current year has increased by 60% compared to 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the uptake of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: I am pleased to say that uptake for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme continues to exceed our expectations.  As of 10 December 2024, over 14,000 children were registered and approved for the Scheme which we estimate to be over 90% of those eligible. Since the Scheme commenced, working parents have saved over £4m on their childcare costs, with an estimated additional £4.7m saved through Tax Free Childcare. The combined effect being a saving of nearly £9m for working parents in Northern Ireland since September 2024.I appreciate there have been calls to increase the level of the subsidy and extend it to others such as parents with school age children.  These are options which I will be considering over the coming months.  I also intend to bring forward a comprehensive draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.It will take time to implement the Strategy, so my priority in the interim will be to continue and, if budget permits, expand the measures put in place this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has identified an increase in the Schmallenberg virus in sheep flocks in recent months.
answer_text: Schmallenberg virus (SBV) is not a notifiable disease in Northern Ireland (NI). As a result, there are no movement restrictions or controls required. The Department does not take active surveillance measures for this disease. Information held by the Department is limited to cases voluntarily submitted for investigation to Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) and may not be a comprehensive reflection of the NI disease picture in respect of SBV.DAERA advises farmers to contact their Private Veterinary Practitioner (PVP) if they suspect the presence of the disease in their cattle or sheep. Any reported cases of deformed offspring that meet the clinical case definition are investigated by AFBI free of charge. AFBI have reported four positive cases of SBV in sheep since October 2024. This follows five years of negative results in all cases submitted for investigation to AFBI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline measures he intends to take to prevent online fraudulent claims for Universal Credit.
answer_text: A number of measures are in place to manage the risk of online fraudulent claims to Universal Credit, primarily the verification of identity. Those applying for Universal Credit can verify their identity online; the Universal Credit system uses His Majesty’s Revenue & Customs (HMRC) Identity Verification system.Those who are unable to verify their identity online will be required to attend a telephone interview where they will be asked a series personalised questions, or they can bring their identity documents to a Jobs and Benefit office for verification. Whichever method is selected, UC claimants must verify their identity.Other preventative measures include asking new or existing claimants to provide additional information to verify their declared circumstances.   The Universal Credit system interfaces with other government systems, including the Home Office and HMRC. The sharing of this information provides an added means of detecting fraud within Universal Credit claims.My Department has a robust strategy in place to tackle benefit fraud and acts on emerging risks on an ongoing basis to ensure that resources are deployed effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14622/22-27, for his assessment of when solely looking at the recycling rate derived from the kerbside collection of dry recyclables, that the NI Local Authority Collection Municipal Waste Statistics 2022/2023 highlight that local councils with commingled collection including glass perform better.
answer_text: Several councils that operate commingled collection systems, including glass, have demonstrated strong recycling rates. However, recycling rates are calculated for entire council areas, and no council currently has all households using a kerbside sort system. When we focus solely on dry recycling rates, it appears that commingled collections with glass perform better.As highlighted in my response to AQW 15594/22-27, recycling rates are influenced by a range of factors, with the type of dry recycling service offered being only one element of overall performance. While commingled collections can yield higher recycling rates, they may also face challenges such as contamination and reduced material quality. These factors need to be balanced against the increased volume of recyclables collected.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: The Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA) published alongside the Rethinking Our Resources consultation document includes a range of options with costs, including initial transition and ongoing operating costs.Following analysis of the responses to the consultation, any policy options I bring forward will be fully costed, with data updated where necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14624/22-27, for his assessment of whether the Regulatory Impact Assessment, to which he referred, does not include a detailed cost model.
answer_text: The recent Rethinking Our Resources consultation included a range of proposals for household and non-household municipal waste. The proposals were collaboratively designed to help Northern Ireland achieve the challenging recycling and carbon reduction targets set out in legislation.The costs of transition for these proposals are outlined in the Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA). A detailed cost modelling approach was used through direct engagement with key stakeholders and examined several options under consideration and proposed in the consultation. The detailed cost model is not included in full within the RIA due to the commercially sensitive nature of the information.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14622/22-27, whether he accepts that recycling rates are higher among commingled household waste management systems than that of kerbside sort box systems.
answer_text: To facilitate our transition to a low carbon, circular economy, it is necessary that we improve on not only the quantity, but also the quality of our recycling and retain as much of that recycling for processing locally within our own economy. The published recycling rates do not account for the quality or end destinations of the material collected.The Local Authority Collected (LAC) Municipal Waste statistics for 2022/23, published by my department show that recycling rates for dry recycling collected in kerbside sort systems are similar to that of commingled household waste management systems. The relatively small number of households that use a kerbside sort system compared to the number of those with a commingled system means that a like for like comparison of recycling rates is inappropriate.There are many local contextual variables which influence recycling rates including housing stock, deprivation, and garden size, for example, as well as scheme design variables such as how much residual waste capacity there is available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water is unable to adopt treatment plants due to a lack of funding for maintenance costs.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that it will continue to consider applications for adoptable wastewater treatment works when it is both economically feasible and the preferred strategic solution to service the development site.  Applications for the adoption of wastewater treatment works will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of the new acute mental health facility at Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex.
answer_text: The planned acute mental health facility at Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex is included within my Department's 15-year capital plan.  An outline business case was submitted by Western Health and Social Care Trust in February 2023 and, following Departmental Review, Western Trust is currently revising the business case to reflect changes in mental health staffing arrangements and other strategic needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13498/22-27, to detail (i) the cost model that has been produced for the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation; (i) the full capital expenditure assumptions over the next seven years; and (iii) the operating cost assumptions over the next seven years.
answer_text: Costs for proposals within the Rethinking Our Resources consultation are included in the Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA). This includes transitional costs and ongoing operational costs for the top 7 performing scenarios which were modelled. The RIA can be accessed on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13498/22-27, whether the carbon impact of the proposed waste management system in the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation is more environmentally detrimental than the carbon impact which will result from a fully commingled waste management system.
answer_text: There is no one waste management system proposed in the recent consultation Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI.  Rather, the consultation included a range of proposals designed to improve the quality and quantity of recycling here and sought stakeholder views on these.  Those responses are currently being analysed and will help inform any future policy direction.The greenhouse gas emissions analysis is outlined in the Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA), published alongside the consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 13498/22-27, in relation to data from recycling centres and bottle banks, for his assessment of whether commingled waste management systems have the highest kerbside recycling rates in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is no direct relationship between recycling centres and bottle banks and commingled recycling performance. Recycling centres and bottle banks are situated in locations across Northern Ireland and material is kept separate, not in a mixed or commingled form. The most recent annual recycling statistics come from the Northern Ireland Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Management publication for the period 1 April 2022 – 31 March 2023. It shows that Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council recorded the highest recycling rate of 60.1% for household waste during this period.  59% of homes in Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council currently use a kerbside sort waste collection service.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline when the Northern Ireland Football Fund will be open for applications from interested groups.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether local councils can continue to use the fully co-mingled recycling system if his Department introduces the new waste management and recycling laws proposed by the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation.
answer_text: My Department currently has no powers to direct local councils as to which collection system they use. However, the Waste (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 revoked Section 21 of the Waste Regulations (NI) 2011, which had stated that commingling (collecting all recyclable material in one bin for kerbside collection) was an acceptable form of separate collection. This means local councils (and waste collectors more generally) have a legal obligation to consider collection methods from a place of greatest separation of material streams, only deviating from this through a test which determines comparable quality alongside the technical, environmental, and economic practicability (TEEP) of collection methods.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department can provide the relevant funding for the proposed costs from the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation.
answer_text: The current consultation will close for responses on 27 June 2024, following which,  a period of analysis will follow.  It is only then that I will be in a position to consider final policy options, including associated implementation costs and potential funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the cost of the implementation of proposals listed in the Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI consultation.
answer_text: Costs for proposals within the Rethinking Our Resources consultation are included in the Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA), published alongside the consultation on the DAERA website.  The RIA modelled 21 collection scheme scenarios to determine costs and performance. It indicates that effective implementation of the top performing scenarios could see Northern Ireland achieve a recycling rate of 74%.  For households, initial capital investment and transition costs for all eleven local councils have been modelled at between £66 - £101 million in the RIA, however local councils could also see ongoing cost savings of between £2 - £21 million per annum (depending on the collection scheme).For the non-household municipal sector, no direct support for transition costs has been proposed. Rather, following the outcome of the consultation, my Department plans to launch a campaign entitled “The Business of Recycling” which will provide guidance, tools, and support for those within the sector. The estimated cost of this is £18 million.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what level of business support her Department will provide to the childcare sector.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of lessons they have learned for their Department arising from the evidence presented to the Northern Ireland module of the Covid-19 Inquiry.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11497/22-27, to detail his proposed approach to the Planning Appeals Commission following their decision to suspend the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry.
answer_text: The proposed approach of my Department is set out in correspondence to the Planning Appeals Commission, and their associated responses, are available on the PACNI website at Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian). | Planning Appeals Commission (pacni.gov.uk). 		My Officials will continue to co-operate fully with the PACNI on these matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the last review of the Office for Environmental Protection was carried out.
answer_text: The OEP’s remit extended to NI in February 2022, after Assembly approval. Given the short time frame since their establishment there has been no review of the OEP.However, the OEP produces an annual report on the exercise of its functions which is laid before the Assembly and published.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of funding his Department has provided the Office for Environmental Protection in each of the last three years.
answer_text: In its first full year of operation in NI, 2022-23, the OEP received £670k resource funding from my Department, but was unable to spend its full allocation and returned £71k.  In 2023-24, my Department provided an increased resource allocation of £1,250k.   Allocations for 2024-25 have not yet been finalised and will depend on available budgets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the environmental bodies operating under his Department; and (ii) the annual cost of each body in each of the last three years.
answer_text: DAERA and its Arm Length Bodies and agencies carry responsibility for environment, climate change and sustainability, food, farming, fisheries, forestry, and the development of the rural sector in NI. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is an Executive Agency within the DAERA operating with the primary purpose of protecting and enhancing Northern Ireland’s environment, and in doing so, deliver health and well-being benefits and support economic growth. NIEA expenditure in the last 3 years is:2021/22 - £39.43m2022/23 - £33.91m2023/24 - £33.75m
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the environmental areas covered by the work of the Office for Environmental Protection.
answer_text: The role of the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP), as set out in the Environment Act 2021, is to contribute to the protection and improvement of the natural environment by holding government and other public authorities to account. It does so by: scrutinising Environmental Improvement Plans and environmental law; advising government departments on environmental law; and, where necessary, taking enforcement action on serious breaches of environmental law. It is also worth noting that the OEP must not monitor the implementation of, or report on, a matter within the remit of the Committee on Climate Change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
