question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Planning Appeals Commission sought legal advice when it decided not to proceed with the scheduled opening hearing sessions of the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry in September 2024.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what respite care is available for young people with complex needs and severe learning difficulties, who are being cared for at home full-time.
answer_text: Short Break and respite care provide valuable opportunities for children & young people with complex needs to spend time away from their parents / primary carers, whilst also allowing parents / primary carers to have a break from their caring responsibilities. Across Northern Ireland respite care for young people with complex needs and severe learning difficulties is delivered by the five Health and Social Care Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector. Access to and the frequency of short breaks is dependent on individually assessed need.Short Break provision in Northern Ireland for young people with complex needs and severe learning difficulties who are being cared for at home may comprise of: overnight periods of care in a short break residential unit; day or overnight periods of care with an approved carer; support / care services provided to the young person in their home that enables the family to engage with other tasks; befriending services, which may be aimed at undertaking specific activities with the young person; community activities, including after-school, evening and weekend group activities (i.e. youth groups); day trips and holidays; summer schemes.Short Breaks and respite services are also provided by the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice for all children with a life limiting condition across the five HSC Trusts. My department officials continue to work with the NI Children’s Hospice to plan for a more sustainable future.It is acknowledged that regionally availability for young people with disability is a pressure across the HSC Trusts.There has been an increase in children & young people reported to have disability and there has been an increasing demand for access to short breaks & respite services. My Department continues to work with each of the HSC Trusts in terms of closely monitoring regional service provision for both young people and adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the Planning Appeals Commission considered the views of the three referring authorities of the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry when the Commission decided to suspend the start of the inquiry.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 11499/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department, as a referring authority, is in a position to commence the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry on 10 September 2024 as initially planned.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission has decided to suspend arrangement for the current conjoined inquiry timetable. My Department has written to the PAC NI outlining a proposed approach, we await a response to that proposal. The setting of the timetable for the inquiry is entirely for the PAC NI to decide.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department, as a referring authority, is in a position to commence the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry on 10 September 2024 as initially planned.
answer_text: On 22 September 2021 the Department for Infrastructure, pursuant to its powers under s.26 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 caused a public local inquiry into three planning applications. The Department appointed the Commission, as an independent body to examine and to consider representations in respect of those applications. Other government bodies with related consents also caused public local inquiries under their own specific legislation and asked the Commission to hear these in conjunction with the planning applications. The decision to conjoin the various matters was one taken by the Commission.Given issues with some of the abstraction licenses which were to be conjoined with the PLI into the planning applications the Commission has concluded that the hearing sessions will not commence as planned on 10 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the Planning Appeals Commission’s decision to suspend the start of the Curraghinalt Project (Dalradian) Public Inquiry was consistent with the views of the three referring authorities.
answer_text: Whilst the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service has sponsorship responsibility for the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC), the Commission is an independent appellant body. Thus, similarly to the judiciary, it is completely independent of the department in its handling of cases and decision-making.It would not be appropriate for me as Minister of Justice to comment on matters that encroach on the independence of the Chief Commissioner or to provide commentary on or assessments of their decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to introduce legislation to ensure private developers complete roadways and footpaths within a reasonable timeframe.
answer_text: With your permission speaker I would like to answer Question 9 and Question 13 together.The existing Private Streets Order (NI) 1980 allows my Department to exercise its powers throughout the process of planning, construction and adoption of new developments.The Order provides my Department with powers to commence enforcement action using a bond provided by the developer, to fund the completion of roads and footpaths.However, enforcement action can often be a protracted process, and liaison with developers to achieve completion of infrastructure works to an adoptable standard is the preferred approach.  The Department has no plans to introduce further legislation to deal with this issue.I do however, also recognise that there are often complex problems outside of my Department’s control that need to be resolved before the sewerage and roads infrastructure in a development can be adopted.My Roads officials continue to work with colleagues in NI Water to identify, prioritise, and progress the adoption of private development sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what action he intends to take to ensure the Northern Ireland Environment Agency is able to respond to consultations on planning applications within a reasonable timeframe.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee within the planning system DAERA performance is measured against a duty to respond to a statutory consultation within 21 calendar days or otherwise agreed timescale.Indicative figures for 2023/2024 indicate the Agency has responded to 74% of its statutory consultations within the 21-day, or agreed target date.  This is the highest level annual performance rate by my Department in the past 5 years.  The average number of days taken to respond to a consultation was 19 days in 2023/24, a reduction of 19 days in comparison to an average of 38 days in 2020/2021.  Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to improve performance including: a review and update of internal processes, procedures and guidance; analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of reconsultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation;engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements with agents to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation ; andTemporary redeployment of additional caseworkersAdditional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates in order to allow staff to focus on the actual processing of consultations.   In addition, my Department is working in collaboration with the DfI cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum and has contributed to a number of exercises aiming to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.I would like to acknowledge the good work by officials in demonstrating improvement but accept that more is required.  It is recognised that the permanent staff complement is not adequate and further resource is constrained by ongoing budgetary pressures.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide the date of all joint meetings he has held with the Ulster Farmer's Union and the Vetinerary Service to specifically discuss wildlife intervention and the ongoing tuberculosis issue, in the last eight weeks.
answer_text: Since taking up post, including over the last eight weeks, I and my officials have met regularly with a wide range of stakeholders to discuss the issue of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) and the matter of wildlife intervention.  These meetings have included frequent engagement with the Ulster Farmers’ Union (UFU).I met with the UFU Presidential team on 14 February and recently attended the UFU Annual Dinner on 22 March where bTB and wildlife intervention were discussed.  I have also already accepted an invitation from the UFU to undertake a farm visit in the near future to explore further bTB eradication in Northern Ireland.  This will be arranged when my diary permits.I am committed to finding new, affordable, evidence-based ways of tackling bTB and have asked the new Chief Veterinary Officer for Northern Ireland to undertake a review of our current approach to bTB so that I can be satisfied that the approach we are taking is both science-led and cost-effective.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the proposed Strategy for Victims and Survivors.
answer_text: A new draft Strategy has been developed by TEO in co-operation with the Victims and Survivors Service, the Commission for Victims and Survivors and victims’ groups, as well as individual victims and survivors. 		It is intended to cover a ten-year period and will ensure the right trauma informed and victim centred services are in place to support victims and survivors.		As well as setting out the outcomes we want to achieve for victims and survivors.  The new and reviewed strategy will build on the progress we have made to date to ensure their needs continue to be met.Officials are currently reviewing responses to the public consultation which closed on 29 March, after which we will receive an updated strategy for our consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what information his Department holds in relation to backyard breeders of XL Bully dogs; and (ii) what action he plans to take to stop them.
answer_text: Any dog breeding establishment, which is operating on a commercial basis, will require a licence from the relevant council in which they are located. A person who keeps only one or two breeding bitches is classified as a hobby breeder and does not have to obtain a dog breeding licence. Consequently, my department does not hold any information on any dog breeding premises. Councils are also responsible for enforcement of dog breeding rules and investigating reports of illegal breeding of all dogs, including XL Bully types. I am acutely aware of the need for animal welfare reform across a range of areas. I am committed to reviewing animal welfare legislation and will be engaging with my officials to establish priorities and to determine what legislation can be delivered within resource constraints and the lifetime of the current mandate.   While I have no current plans for a formal review of legal and illegal dog breeding, I am conscious that more action may be required in the time ahead and so I am keen to work with stakeholders on this and other related issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of attacks that have taken place by XL Bully dogs in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the number of these attacks that were fatal, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has been liaising with local councils, who are responsible for enforcing dog control legislation, and sought the information requested.  Councils have advised that they have not historically recorded the breed of dog involved in attacks here.  However, 5 councils have informed my Department that 16 attacks have been attributed to XL Bully type dogs, with at least 7 of those occurring in the last 12 months.  None of the attacks were fatal.The number of licenced XL Bully dogs in Northern Ireland has increased by over 50% in the last six months, following the announcement in September 2023 by the UK Government that these dogs would be subject to a ban in England and Wales.  This followed a series of 24 fatal attacks in Great Britain, many of which were attributed to XL Bully type dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of women in Northern Ireland born in the 1950s affected by the increase in the State Pension age; (ii) whether his Department has had any communication with the Department of Works and Pensions in relation to compensation for these women; and (iii) whether his Department has considered any proposals to compensate these women for the financial loss they have sustained.
answer_text: The number of women in Northern Ireland born in the 1950s affected by the changes in State pension age is estimated to be 100,700. The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman report into how changes to women’s State pension age were communicated by DWP was published on 21 March 2024. I await the Westminster Government’s response to that report.In the meantime, my officials will continue to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the areas his Department's officials continue to provide checks at Northern Ireland ports in relation to the Northern Ireland Protocol.
answer_text: My Department delivers checks on animals, plants and agri-food goods as required by EU Regulation 2017/625 (Official Controls Regulation (OCR)), which applies in Northern Ireland by virtue of the Windsor Framework. However, recognising Northern Ireland’s unique situation, the Windsor Framework Agreement, and subsequent EU Regulation 2023/1231 (SPS Regulation) introduce derogation from specific provisions within OCR. These include the adoption of specific rules relating to the entry of consignments of retail goods and specific rules for plants for planting, seed potatoes, machinery and certain vehicles operated for agricultural or forestry purposes, as well as non-commercial movements of certain pet animals from Great Britain into Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when checks carried out by his Department’s officials at Northern Ireland ports are likely to be reduced as a result of legislation passed by the UK government.
answer_text: In line with the SPS Regulation (EU) 2023/1231, the Windsor Framework (Retail Movement Scheme) Regulations 2023 provides that identity checks carried out on consignments moving from Great Britian (GB) to Northern Ireland (NI) through the Northern Ireland Retail Movement Scheme will reduce from the current figure of 10% to 8% from 1 October 2024, and to 5 % from 1 July 2025.Any future change to UK legislation is a matter for the UK Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why there are delays for surgery for people on the urgent waiting list for vitrectomy surgery; and (ii) how he plans to address this.
answer_text: There are variations in demand for this regional service week to week as patients present via Eye Casualty or other emergency departments. The Belfast Trust ophthalmic surgical scheduling team liaise directly with each patient on the urgent list and advise them individually of the expected timeframe within which they will be offered a surgical slot.Due to variation in demand levels the waiting times fluctuate between three to ten days and patients are kept informed during this time. Patients requiring urgent vitrectomy surgery are assigned in chronological order.I have been advised that a recent retiree surgeon has been replaced by the Belfast Trust and an additional surgical list has been assigned to this service weekly. It is anticipated that both of these actions will improve the waiting time when in place. This is expected to be operational from the summer of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will carry out a review of the criteria used for the Boiler Replacement Scheme to enable low paid families not in receipt of benefits to avail of the scheme.
answer_text: My department has no plans to re-open or review the criteria for the Boiler Replacement Scheme.  The current Affordable Warmth Scheme has approval to run until March 2026 and includes providing grants for central heating systems.  The Scheme aims to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. Free comprehensive advice and support on how to save energy in the home and eligibility for help to improve energy efficiency is available from the NI Energy Advice Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what initiatives he plans to bring forward to increase job opportunities in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Creating opportunities for good jobs across the North is central to my vision for a regionally balanced economy. Invest NI will have a particularly important role in helping to achieve this, and I have tasked the organisation with increasing its focus and resources at a sub-regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the health service has a body scanner for the detection of melanoma; and (ii) to detail the location of these scanners.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust has a Mole Mapping scanner for the detection of melanoma, located in Ward 25 at the Ulster Hospital, Dundonald.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the recently announced £1 million to repair potholes will be allocated to DfI Western division.
answer_text: In total Western Division received £250,000 of the £1 million allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how he plans to reduce cancer treatment waiting times.
answer_text: The delays being experienced by patients across many of the areas of our health service are unacceptable, but that is especially the case in time critical treatments such as cancer.  At present, delays in the diagnostic pathway are the primary reason for delays in patients commencing treatment. The Cancer Strategy recognises that the current system is unsustainable and that transformational change is urgently needed. Everyone in Northern Ireland should have equitable, and timely, access to cancer diagnosis and treatment. Last week I told Executive colleagues the funding requirements needed to tackle our waiting lists. Minimum funding of approximately £75-80m per year in needed to ensure those with cancer or time critical conditions can be treated as required. But as important as those non-recurrent payments are, what our health service desperately needs is knowing not only what money it has next week or next month, but in the next number of years. The Cancer Strategy has invested an additional £11.3m yearly into Cancer Services. This has supported the stabilisation of oncology and haematology services, and the development of Rapid Diagnosis Centres. It is essential that we ensure that cancer diagnostic and treatment pathways are as efficient as possible. My department has recently completed reviews of the three core haematological cancer pathways, and is working to implement the recommendations. Work is also underway to implement the optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer.An urgent review of regional breast cancer services has commenced, while work is also underway to address significant pressures in Systemic Anti-Cancer Treatment, and Head and Neck Cancer services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support he plans to offer families who are struggling with childcare costs.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to the Assembly on 12th February and Executive colleagues on 15th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  However, I am conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place and I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet parents, providers, and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand the issues which they are facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his plans to address the social housing need in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Within West Tyrone there are currently 1,929 applicants on the waiting list for the allocation of a social home.Of these 1,341 are deemed to be in housing stress. While 187 homes have been allocated over the last year, I am acutely aware that there is a shortage in the supply of homes which needs to be addressed.Within the West Tyrone constituency, the projected housing need is for 613 new social homes over the period 2022-2027.Within the constituency there have been 30 new social housing properties completed to-date in 2023/24. 346 social housing properties are currently under construction. This includes 75 confirmed Starts in 2023/24 to-date within the Constituency. There are 195 social housing properties programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme 2023/24 & 2024/25 – 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what impact the increase in price of building materials has had on planned maintenance and other schemes within the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In October 2022, the Housing Executives Board took the decision to release 10 contractors from its CT055 planned maintenance contracts due to the significant inflationary increases in cost being experienced at the time. The Housing Executive has procured replacement contracts which commenced in September 2023 and are currently delivering works. In terms of other planned & response maintenance schemes, we acknowledged there had been significant increased costs in terms of materials and labour in the construction industry which has had an impact on service delivery. To support our contractors and the construction industry, from 2021 we have provided evidence-based uplifts within the confines of the contracts and The Public Contract Regulations 2015.  The Housing Executive’s Private Sector Improvement Services team is acutely aware of the challenges faced by contractors in delivering building works and regularly reviews its Schedule of Rates to ensure that the grant-aid approval value reflects fair market rates.  The most recent review was agreed in March 2024 when a rate of 8.646% was applied to Home Improvement Grants rates and 8.0% to that of the Affordable Warmth Scheme.The Housing Executive trust this recent uplift will help grant-aid customers, and their chosen contractor, progress the necessary works towards completion.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many properties have been excluded by Project Stratum due to the Land and Property Services recording of premises being built and approved at the time of the Project Stratum Open Market Review, broken down by area.
answer_text: Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Megabits per second or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The Project Stratum target intervention area was defined following an Open Market Review (OMR), during which my Department compiled a list of eligible premises using the Pointer dataset maintained by Land & Property Services. I can confirm that no premises were excluded from the project due to a Pointer classification of Built and Approved.The contract to deliver Project Stratum was awarded to Fibrus Networks in November 2020. Following contract award, my Department became aware of premises which were not considered for Project Stratum because, within the version of Pointer that was made available to my Department, these premises had not been categorised as Built and Approved. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many first-aid responders, designated to treat staff who are victims of assault and injury in HM Prison Maghaberry, are on duty daily.
answer_text: While there are 26 staff trained as first aid responders in Maghaberry prison, not all will be on duty each day.In addition to our first aid responders, the healthcare professionals from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust who work in Maghaberry will also assist if they are required to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps has she taken or plans to take to ensure the health and safety of all prison officers at HM Prison Maghaberry.
answer_text: The Health and Safety of all prison staff is a priority that both the Director General and I take very seriously.  Prisons are by nature complex operational environments in which we accommodate many of society’s most vulnerable and challenging members.  In managing prisons, Governors are required to undertake daily dynamic risk assessments which include reviewing staffing levels, ensuring essential services are delivered and assessing the level of regime that can be provided to facilitate the rehabilitation of those in our care.  Such assessments allow the Governor to make balanced decisions in relation to staff deployment, the level of regime which can be offered and what activities and services can be delivered.In taking such decisions it is the Governors priority is to ensure that the health and safety of staff is maintained and that those in our care our accommodated in a way that is safe, decent and secure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what is the current staff shortages in HM Prison Maghaberry during (i) weekdays; and (ii) weekends.
answer_text: As HMP Maghaberry is a high security facility, it would not be appropriate to comment on specific staffing levels as to do so could impact upon the safe and secure operation of the prison.Overall the Northern Ireland Prison Service is currently managing a significant increase in the prison population and a higher than normal level of staff absence, due to a combination of sickness absence and absence as a result of COVID-19.  As a result Governors are required to undertake daily dynamic risk assessments which include reviewing staffing levels, ensuring essential services are delivered and assessing the level of regime that can be provided to facilitate the rehabilitation of those in our care.  Such assessments allow the Governor to make balanced decisions in relation to staff deployment, the level of regime that can be offered and what activities and services can be delivered each day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce HPV Primary Screening.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what is the cause for the delay in the development of the Strule Shared Education Campus in Omagh.
answer_text: The delay in the development of the Strule Shared Education Campus was initially caused by tendering issues and later the need for business case re-approval and funding confirmation following the suspension of the Main Works Contract procurement in 2018.  More recently the Covid-19 pandemic also impacted on progress.The previous Main Works procurement competition has been closed and the Department has now formally commenced a fresh Main Works procurement competition with the release of the Invitation to Tender on 13 December 2021. On the current programme timetable, assuming successful appointment of a main works contractor and following the pre-construction design period, campus construction is due to begin on site in the spring of 2023 and is currently planned to complete early 2026, with potential partial opening of some facilities in academic year 2025/26 and full campus opening planned for September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why a timetable has not yet been provided by the Planning Appeals Commission for the public inquiry into the proposed Dalradian Gold Mine project, which was referred in September 2021.
answer_text: The Department has now transferred all the information requested by the Planning Appeals Commission in relation to this project. The handling and timing of the Public Inquiry is a matter for the Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the recent publication of reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office and the Public Accounts Committee on Planning in Northern Ireland, what measures (i) have been implemented; and (ii) will be implemented, by the Planning Appeals Commission to ensure Regionally Significant planning applications are progressed in a timely manner.
answer_text: The two reports to which you refer looked at the performance of the Department of Infrastructure (DfI) and Local Councils. The Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) was not included in the scope of either report. PAC is an independent tribunal body with case management and progression a function of the Chief Commissioner. It would therefore not be appropriate for me, as Minister of Justice, to comment on matters that encroach on that independence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proportion of the South West Acute Hospital is currently not being utilised.
answer_text: Less than 5% of the commissioned space in the South West Acute Hospital is currently not being utilised.  The South West Acute Hospital site has a range of clinical and non-clinical areas enabling the delivery of healthcare services, and also has provision of on-site accommodation for students/staff and a creche.  The Trust continues to review the utilisation of space within the South West Acute Hospital to ensure that it is optimised in support of changing patterns of service delivery to meet the needs of our patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress of the development of the Acute Mental Health Facility at the new local Enhanced Hospital in Omagh.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust submitted a proposal for Phase 2 Development of Omagh (Centre for Mental Health) as part of a recent capital planning review exercise for the period 2022 - 2032. Confirmation of funding and timescales is dependent on Business Case approval and allocation of capital funding by my Department. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.  However, my ability to strategically plan capital investment is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to install charging points for electric vehicles in villages in West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department has been leading on the Transport Working Group (TWG), set up to inform the transport elements of the Executive’s Energy Strategy. The Energy Strategy, which was published in Dec 2021, includes actions that need to be taken forward if we are to achieve a cleaner, greener transport system. The out workings of the TWG will include consideration of how best to support vehicle decarbonisation within a sustainable transport hierarchy, including future requirements for publicly available electric vehicle charge points in the North. I am keen to work within the framework of the Energy Strategy to ensure a co-ordinated and coherent approach which is focused on assessing and meeting future needs for charging infrastructure in this fast-changing policy area. My Department has set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network in the North. ESB can be contacted directly, regarding how many charging points for electric vehicles are currently in (a) Omagh; and (b) Strabane and its plans to increase the number of charge points across the North including villages in West Tyrone, at ecars@esb.ie or enquiries can be sent directly to ESB, Two Gateway, East Wall Road, DUBLIN 3, D03 A995. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am also pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. My Department will be contributing €513,945 (£455,371) of Match Funding towards the FASTER project. The responsibility for the implementation of the project rests with SEUPB and it has agreed a delivery schedule with the project partners, led by the East Border Region Group Limited (EBR). It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North and EBR is best placed to advise on charge point locations.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). My Department has made match-funding available of circa £350k, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Sc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what respite provisions are available in the Western Health and Social Care Trust for those who care for family members with adult learning disabilities; and how frequently families can access these facilities.
answer_text: Short Breaks remains limited as Trusts are dependent on access to respite beds in care homes, which are subject to public health guidance in relation to admissions from the community. The Department has commissioned the Public Health Agency to review admission criteria to improve access to Short Breaks services. Additionally, Trusts have reported the some care home providers have withdrawn respite beds during the pandemic, which have not been reopened to Trusts. Western Trust has continued to undertake assessments of need in respect of Short Breaks and has continued to offer both traditional bed-based Short Breaks and Direct Payments in lieu of Short Breaks. The latter particularly being offered in those situations where access to a Short Breaks has not been available. Due to the limited availability of bed based Short Breaks over the last two years the frequency of access to this service has been determined by the social work assessment of need.  The service holds fortnightly panels to allocate places within the Short Breaks service.   Allocation of traditional break beds is carried out as equitable as possible so that carers have some level of short break provision.Please see below table detailing the beds available in each facility.Provision of Short Break Services for Adults with Learning DisabilityTypes of Short BreaksFacility/ServiceBeds/PlacesStatutory Residential RespiteThe Adult Respite Cottage, LondonderrySpruce House, AltnagelvinBeltany House, Omagh4 beds2 beds3 beds Independent Nursing HomesOwen Mor Nursing Home, LondonderryPrivate Nursing homes within the WHSCT area4 bedsVacant beds can be accessed for short breaks as and when  available and within available fundingIndependent Residential HomesDrumary House, DerrygonnellyPrivate Residential homes within the WHSCT area1 BedVacant beds can be accessed for short breaks as and when  available and within available fundingShort-term Adult Placement ServiceOvernight and day hours are  provided by Host CarersThe Trust has a number of host carers providing this service across the Trust areaDirect PaymentsRecurring and one-off direct payments are made directly to parents/carers within available fundingDomiciliary CareNight Sitting Service – A small number of  families are currently receiving this service

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the definition of an acute hospital.
answer_text: An acute hospital is a hospital that delivers acute care.  Acute care covers a broad range of services provided to patients who require treatment for a period of time for a brief but serious illness, injury or other health condition, and will generally include services such as accident and emergency departments, inpatient and outpatient medicine and surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 63/22-27, why no record is kept on follow up cancer patient appointments.
answer_text: Waiting list data provided to the Department of Health via the HSC Data Warehouse are for patients waiting for first consultant-led outpatient appointments, including those with referral reasons related to suspected cancer. The Department is granted access to these data in order to produce National Statistics on waiting times, support policy development and to inform the setting of and report against Ministerial targets.The management and scheduling of follow-up appointments for cancer patients is the responsibility of individual Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Departmental Officials last met with Fermanagh and Omagh District Council to discuss the potential development of the site at St. Lucia Barracks.
answer_text: Officials have met both elected members and officers from Fermanagh and Omagh District Council on a number of occasions to discuss the St Lucia site, the most recent meeting was with the Council’s Omagh Place Shaping Group on 23 May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: I am committed to doing all that I can during my time as Minister for Infrastructure to advance this vitally important flagship project.The consultation period on the recently published Environmental Statement Addendum and associated documentation concluded on 6 May 2022, with the Department receiving over 200 written representations.Discussions are ongoing with the PAC and it is hoped that the public inquiry can be reconvened in the autumn of this year. Receipt of the PAC’s final report from the inquiry process should then allow a new formal decision on progression of the scheme during 2023. Subject to this decision and successful completion of the necessary statutory processes and environmental assessments, the construction of phase 1A (New Buildings to North of Strabane) could then commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are being brought forward for the further upgrade of the A32 between Omagh and Fermanagh.
answer_text: A route improvement strategy for the A32 Omagh to Enniskillen identified a number of upgrade schemes. To date, my Department has delivered two schemes along this route: an on-line improvement at Drumskinny and an off-line realignment at Shannaragh. Of the remaining schemes that were identified, the proposed off-line realignment at Cornamuck is at the most advanced stage.  The design and development work, together with the necessary statutory processes, have all been completed. My Department has also progressed to Preferred Route stage on another proposed scheme at Kilgortnaleague. The further progression of these schemes is subject to a number of factors, including the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's work at St. Lucia Barracks, Omagh.
answer_text: The St Lucia site remains in joint ownership between my Department and the Ministry of Defence (MOD) who retain ownership of the Historic Core.  My officials continue discussions with MOD colleagues regarding the transfer of this remaining part of the site, however it is unlikely that any additional resource will be offered with the transfer.  You will be aware that the purpose of the gifting of this site was to generate revenue for the NI Executive.  However the transfer of the Historic Core in its current condition and given the current market conditions is likely to place a considerable financial burden on the Executive and be disproportionate to its market value.  Notwithstanding this, my officials will continue to explore options with key stakeholders for the regeneration and future use of the St Lucia site so that it can be brought back into active use.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to bring forward a review of the planning system to make it more efficient.
answer_text: My Department has been working in collaboration with councils and other key stakeholders for some time to improve the planning system in a number of ways. These include a cross-governmental planning forum involving local government planning authorities and statutory consultees working together to deliver and oversee continuous improvement in the planning process. The Department has also completed a review into the implementation of the Planning Act which has resulted in 16 recommendations for legislative change or amendment with a view to making the system more effective and efficient. In addition, my Department has also been working with local government to deliver a new regional planning IT system which will go live in the autumn. It has also recently published the report of the Planning Engagement Partnership containing recommendations on how to enhance the quality and depth of community engagement in the planning process at both the regional and local planning levels. The Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) report, published on 1 February 2022 and the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report, published on 22 March 2022, have produced a number of recommendations in relation to improving the planning system in the North. My Department has already commenced work and engagement with other stakeholders in relation to these recommendations and actions, and will continue to work collaboratively with councils going forward to deliver both strategic and operational improvements to the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans does Invest NI have for future investment in West Tyrone.
answer_text: Invest NI continues to provides a range of financial and practical support to help new business start-up, indigenous business growth across all of Northern Ireland including the West Tyrone (WT) area as well as promoting the region globally as an inward investment location.  Over the last 5 years Invest NI has supported businesses with £5.54m of employment-related assistance which will leverage a total investment of £57.86m.  In addition, Invest NI provided 523 offers of other developmental assistance to businesses, totalling £14.2m, to accelerate their further growth including financial assistance for research and development, skills development and to support the promotion of products and services made in the West Tyrone constituency in overseas markets. The resourcing and delivery of economic development activities has necessitated a higher level of collaboration and partnership working than in the past. A collaborative delivery approach to recovery is essential, with all economic development partners, including Councils, Chambers of Commerce, and Academia, to mention a few, adapting their suite of support to aid future growth.To this end the agency has helped shape the key actions within the economic pillars of all 11 Community Plans including those for the Fermanagh & Omagh District Council area and the Derry City & Strabane District Council area. Similarly, my Department and Invest NI continue to work with the Councils and other relevant stakeholders on the emerging projects within the Mid-South West Growth Deal (MSW) and the Derry~Londonderry & Strabane Region City Deal (DSCD). City/Growth Deals provide a significant opportunity for economic growth across the region. They can play an integral part of wider economic / societal enhancement programs led by both Councils and offers the opportunity of delivering real improvements across all segments of society. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many inward investment visits have Invest NI undertaken in the constituency of West Tyrone, in the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of visits by potential foreign direct investors hosted by Invest NI which took place in the constituency of West Tyrone in each of the years between 2017/18 and 2021/22.	Constituency	17/18	18/19	19/20	20/21	21/22	West Tyrone	0	2	1	0	0			Number of VisitsDue to the Covid-19 pandemic restrictions, Invest NI pivoted to a virtual visit delivery model for potential inward investor company visits as of April 2020. Therefore, a breakdown of all visits by potential inward investors per Parliamentary Council Area for the financial year 2020-2021 is not applicable. In 2021-2022 Invest NI continued to offer virtual visits for potential inward investors as well as physical visits. It should be noted that physical visits only took place when government restrictions permitted and all necessary precautions were implemented.Invest NI approachInvest NI promotes the whole of Northern Ireland as an attractive and viable location for inward investment opportunities to new investors, along with the parent companies of existing foreign owned investors.   Northern Ireland’s investment proposition is therefore promoted on the basis of a talented and loyal workforce, advanced telecoms infrastructure, high skilled business clusters, a pro-business environment, competitive operating costs and proximity to major markets.The requirements will vary depending on the nature of the project, but a company will typically look at existing investors in the same business sector, universities and colleges that offer courses relating to that sector; and suitable, available property. An area with a profile that clearly and closely aligns to the needs of investors across a range of locational factors is better placed to attract potential investor exploratory visits. Potential investors are also often drawn to population centres that they consider will provide the appropriate number of suitably skilled potential employees.  Therefore a clear understanding and evidence of skill demographics for any region would greatly assist a potential investor in considering a particular area. Ultimately the investor will make the decision as to where they visit and locate based on their specific business needs and having considered the options available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times in each Health and Social Care Trust for a first appointment for cancer patients.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the change in waiting times in each Health and Social Care Trust for cancer patient first appointments over the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Health what action each Health and Social Care Trust has taken in the last 12 months to reduce waiting times for cancer patients.
answer_text: All Trusts have taken a number of actions in order to minimise / reduce cancer waiting times over the past 12 months including:1.	Optimising capacity, both in house and in the independent sector (IS), through the waiting list initiative funding (£90m non-recurrent funding for elective waiting lists in 2021/22). Trusts are utilising IS for first appointments, day case procedures, endoscopy and radiology where possible.2.	Outpatient appointments have been changed from face to face to virtual in some specialties in order to optimise capacity, to include provision of video consultations.3.	Conversion of routine appointments to red flag appointments in some specialties or clinics (e.g. breast assessment) to ensure red flags are prioritised within the available capacity.4.	Development and delivery of Rebuild plans which include rebuilding of outpatient and surgical provision back to pre COVID levels (subject to ongoing IPC and testing requirements associated with COVID).   Surgery currently at 70% of pre-COVID levels.5.	Systemic Anti Cancer Therapy  services have been maintained throughout  the pandemic through the use  virtual appointments where possible to reduce foot fall and use of home delivery of oral SACT when appropriate.  However, COVID has led to an increase in demand and waits have extended in some areas.  Significant investment is being made in oncology-haematology stabilisation (£4.8m invested in 2021/22 with further investment planned in 2022/23, subject to funding). 	In addition to the above there have been a number of regional initiatives aimed at minimising waits. These include:-	A Regional Prioritisation Oversight Group meets weekly to prioritise available surgical capacity to most urgent patients to include sharing of theatre list across Trusts in order to maximise theatre utilisation on all sites. -	The introduction of quantitative faecal immunohistochemistry test (qFIT) into primary care to aid identification of patients at higher risk of bowel cancer, and enabling Trusts to prioritise available scopes capacity according to patient risk.-	4 additional nurse endoscopy sessions recurrently funded in WHSCT.-	Work is ongoing to develop regional elective centres to increase capacity for critical cancer diagnostics such as cystoscopies and endoscopies. Current plans seek to provide regional endoscopy capacity at both LVH and Omagh; subject to recruitment this would provide for additional 7,500 patients per year.-	15 additional CT sessions per week made available for regional use.-	In addition to securing capacity to see and treat patients in independent sector facilities both within Northern Ireland and in the Republic of Ireland, a number of private healthcare providers are providing in-sourcing services whereby privately recruited teams of clinicians are providing services for HSC patients using available HSC infrastructure.
